items.name: common-paper-cloud-service-agreement
items.description: A cloud service agreement with order form and framework terms, based on Common Paper's standard terms. Covers SaaS subscriptions, payment, SLAs, liability, and data processing. Includes full Standard Terms v2.1.
items.attribution_text: Based on the Common Paper Cloud Service Agreement, available at https://commonpaper.com. Licensed under CC BY 4.0. Copyright Common Paper, Inc.

fields.name: provider_name
fields.description: Name of the cloud service provider

fields.name: customer_name
fields.description: Name of the customer

fields.name: provider_legal_name
fields.description: Official legal entity name of provider (for signature block)

fields.name: customer_legal_name
fields.description: Official legal entity name of customer (for signature block)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective Date of Key Terms referenced in Order Form

fields.name: cloud_service
fields.description: Description of the cloud service / product

fields.name: order_date_is_last_signature
fields.description: If true, order date is date of last signature; if false, use custom_order_date

fields.name: custom_order_date
fields.description: Custom start date (only used when order_date_is_last_signature is false)

fields.name: has_pilot
fields.description: Whether a pilot/trial period is included

fields.name: pilot_period
fields.description: Length of pilot/trial period (e.g. "3 months")

fields.name: pilot_is_free
fields.description: If true, pilot is a free trial; if false, pilot has a fee

fields.name: pilot_fee
fields.description: Fee for pilot period (e.g. "$500"). Only used when pilot_is_free is false

fields.name: pilot_modifications
fields.description: Modifications to the Agreement that apply only during the Pilot Period

fields.name: subscription_period
fields.description: Length of access to the service (e.g. "12 months", "1 year")

fields.name: fee_is_per_unit
fields.description: Whether fees are per-unit pricing (amount per fee_unit)

fields.description: Subscription fee amount (e.g. "$10,000"). Used in computed fees_display.

fields.name: fee_unit
fields.description: Fee billing unit (e.g. "year", "month", "User"). Used in computed fees_display.

fields.name: fee_is_other
fields.description: Whether an alternative fee structure is used

fields.name: other_fee_structure
fields.description: Description of alternative fee structure

fields.name: fee_may_increase
fields.description: Whether fees may increase up to a cap percentage per renewal

fields.name: fee_increase_cap_pct
fields.description: Maximum percentage fees may increase per renewal (e.g. "5")

fields.name: fee_will_increase
fields.description: Whether fees will increase by a fixed percentage per renewal

fields.name: fee_increase_fixed_pct
fields.description: Fixed percentage fees will increase per renewal (e.g. "3")

fields.name: fee_inclusive_of_taxes
fields.description: Whether fees are inclusive of taxes (modifies Standard Terms Section 4.1)

fields.name: payment_by_invoice
fields.description: If true, payment by invoice; if false, automatic payment

fields.name: payment_frequency
fields.description: Billing frequency (e.g. "monthly", "quarterly", "annually")

fields.name: payment_terms_days
fields.description: Days to pay after invoice (e.g. "30", "45"). Only for invoice payment

fields.name: payment_due_from
fields.description: When payment terms start (e.g. "Customer's receipt of invoice")

fields.description: If true, order auto-renews with non-renewal notice; if false, expires at end

fields.name: non_renewal_notice_days
fields.description: Days of notice required before non-renewal (e.g. "30", "60"). Used in computed auto_renewal_display.

fields.description: Geographic restrictions, system requirements, or other use limitations

fields.description: Description of included support and how to access it

fields.description: If true, effective date is date of last signature; if false, use custom date

fields.description: Custom effective date (only used when effective_date_is_last_signature is false)

fields.description: Governing law

fields.description: Jurisdiction
fields.default: courts located in New Castle County, Delaware

fields.name: general_cap_amount
fields.description: General liability cap amount

fields.name: cap_multiplier
fields.description: Liability cap multiplier

fields.name: increased_cap_amount
fields.description: Increased liability cap amount

fields.name: greater_of_dollar
fields.description: Greater-of dollar amount

fields.name: greater_of_multiplier
fields.description: Greater-of multiplier

fields.name: dpa_reference
fields.description: DPA reference

fields.name: pilot_has_fee
fields.description: Set to true when the Pilot Period has an associated fee (not a free trial).

fields.name: pilot_modifications
fields.description: Set to true when modifications to the Agreement apply only during the Pilot Period.

fields.name: fee_is_per_unit
fields.description: Set to true when fees are charged per unit (e.g., per user, per month).

fields.name: fee_is_other
fields.description: Set to true when a custom fee structure applies. Describe in custom_fee_structure.

fields.name: fee_may_increase
fields.description: Set to true when fees may increase up to a specified percentage upon renewal.

fields.name: fee_will_increase
fields.description: Set to true when fees will automatically increase upon renewal.

fields.name: fee_inclusive_of_taxes
fields.description: Set to true when fees are inclusive of taxes, modifying Section 4.1 of the Standard Terms.

fields.name: payment_by_invoice
fields.description: Set to true when payment is by invoice (not automatic payment).

fields.name: no_auto_renew
fields.description: Set to true when this Order Form does not automatically renew, modifying Section 5.1 of the Standard Terms.

fields.name: professional_services_by_reference
fields.description: Set to true when professional services are described by reference to an external SOW or PSA.

fields.name: professional_services_described
fields.description: Set to true when professional services are described inline in the Order Form.

fields.name: professional_services_payment
fields.description: Set to true when a separate payment process applies to professional services.

fields.name: has_provider_covered_claims
fields.description: Set to true when Provider Covered Claims (IP indemnification) are included.

fields.name: has_customer_covered_claims
fields.description: Set to true when Customer Covered Claims (IP and restrictions indemnification) are included.

fields.name: general_cap_type
fields.description: How the General Cap Amount (baseline liability limit) is calculated. "multiplier" uses a multiple of fees, "dollar" uses a fixed amount, "greater_of" uses the greater of a dollar amount or a multiple of fees.

fields.name: increased_cap_type
fields.description: How the Increased Cap Amount (higher liability limit for Increased Claims) is calculated. Same options as general_cap_type.

fields.name: increased_claim_breach_privacy
fields.description: Set to true when breach of Section 3 (Privacy & Security) should be classified as an Increased Claim with a higher liability cap.

fields.name: increased_claim_breach_conf
fields.description: Set to true when breach of Section 10 (Confidentiality) should be classified as an Increased Claim (excluding data or security breaches).

fields.name: increased_claim_indemnification
fields.description: Set to true when indemnification obligations for Covered Claims should be classified as an Increased Claim.

fields.name: increased_claim_breach_privacy_gross
fields.description: Set to true when breach of Privacy & Security resulting from gross negligence is an Increased Claim.

fields.name: increased_claim_breach_conf_gross
fields.description: Set to true when breach of Confidentiality resulting from gross negligence is an Increased Claim.

fields.name: increased_claim_other
fields.description: Set to true to include a custom Increased Claim category. Specify in increased_claim_other_detail.

fields.name: unlimited_claim_indemnification
fields.description: Set to true when indemnification for Covered Claims should have no liability cap (Unlimited Claim).

fields.name: unlimited_claim_breach_privacy_gross
fields.description: Set to true when breach of Privacy & Security resulting from gross negligence should have no liability cap (Unlimited Claim).

fields.name: unlimited_claim_breach_conf_gross
fields.description: Set to true when breach of Confidentiality resulting from gross negligence should have no liability cap (Unlimited Claim).

fields.name: unlimited_claim_breach_privacy
fields.description: Set to true when breach of Section 3 (Privacy & Security) should have no liability cap (Unlimited Claim).

fields.name: unlimited_claim_breach_conf
fields.description: Set to true when breach of Section 10 (Confidentiality) should have no liability cap (Unlimited Claim).

fields.name: unlimited_claim_other
fields.description: Set to true to include a custom Unlimited Claim category. Specify in unlimited_claim_other_detail.

fields.name: additional_warranty_by_provider
fields.description: Set to true when Provider is providing additional warranties beyond the Standard Terms.

fields.name: additional_warranty_by_customer
fields.description: Set to true when Customer is providing additional warranties beyond the Standard Terms.

fields.name: security_reasonable_efforts
fields.description: Set to true when Provider will use commercially reasonable efforts to secure the Cloud Service.

fields.name: has_security_policy
fields.description: Set to true when Provider has a Security Policy available at the specified DPA reference URL.

fields.name: has_security_certifications
fields.description: Set to true when Provider maintains annually updated security reports or certifications.

fields.name: cert_iso_27001
fields.description: Set to true when Provider holds ISO 27001 certification.

fields.name: cert_penetration_testing
fields.description: Set to true when Provider performs regular penetration testing.

fields.name: cert_soc2_type1
fields.description: Set to true when Provider holds SOC 2 Type I certification.

fields.name: cert_pci_level1
fields.description: Set to true when Provider holds PCI Level 1 certification.

fields.name: cert_soc2_type2
fields.description: Set to true when Provider holds SOC 2 Type II certification.

fields.name: cert_pci_level2
fields.description: Set to true when Provider holds PCI Level 2 certification.

fields.name: cert_hitrust
fields.description: Set to true when Provider holds HITRUST certification.

fields.name: cert_fedramp
fields.description: Set to true when Provider holds FedRAMP Authorization.

fields.name: cert_other
fields.description: Set to true to include an additional security certification. Specify the certification in other_security_certification.

fields.name: insurance_commercial_liability
fields.description: Set to true when Provider must carry commercial general liability insurance with a minimum limit.

fields.name: insurance_workers_comp
fields.description: Set to true when Provider must carry workers' compensation or employers' liability insurance.

fields.name: insurance_errors_omissions
fields.description: Set to true when Provider must carry errors and omissions or professional liability insurance.

fields.name: insurance_cyber
fields.description: Set to true when Provider must carry cyber liability insurance with a minimum limit.

fields.name: insurance_additional_insured
fields.description: Set to true when Provider's policies will cover Customer as additional insured.

fields.name: additional_insured_commercial
fields.description: Set to true when Customer is additional insured on commercial general liability policy.

fields.name: additional_insured_eo
fields.description: Set to true when Customer is additional insured on errors and omissions or professional liability policy.

fields.name: additional_insured_cyber
fields.description: Set to true when Customer is additional insured on cyber liability policy.

fields.name: ai_policy_reference
fields.description: Reference to AI usage policy (URL or attached)

fields.name: ai_additional_terms
fields.description: Additional AI-specific terms

fields.name: ai_training_data_none
fields.description: Set to true when no customer data may be used as AI Training Data.

fields.name: ai_training_data_usage
fields.description: Set to true when Usage Data may be used as AI Training Data.

fields.name: ai_training_data_feedback
fields.description: Set to true when Feedback may be used as AI Training Data.

fields.name: ai_training_data_input
fields.description: Set to true when Input may be used as AI Training Data.

fields.name: ai_training_data_output
fields.description: Set to true when Output may be used as AI Training Data.

fields.name: ai_training_data_user_prompts
fields.description: Set to true when User prompts (excluding other Input components) may be used as AI Training Data.

fields.name: ai_training_data_customer_content
fields.description: Set to true when Customer Content may be used as AI Training Data.

fields.name: ai_training_data_other
fields.description: Set to true to include additional AI Training Data categories. Specify in ai_additional_terms.

fields.name: ai_training_data_aggregated
fields.description: Set to true when Training Data must be aggregated before use.

fields.name: ai_training_data_deidentified
fields.description: Set to true when Training Data must be de-identified before use.

fields.name: ai_training_data_deidentify_efforts
fields.description: Set to true when Provider will use commercially reasonable efforts to de-identify Training Data using industry standard technology.

fields.name: ai_training_restrictions_other
fields.description: Set to true to include additional AI Training Data restrictions. Specify in ai_additional_terms.

fields.name: ai_provider_covered_claims
fields.description: Set to true when AI-specific Provider Covered Claims (Output IP infringement) are included in the AI Addendum.

fields.name: ai_customer_covered_claims
fields.description: Set to true when AI-specific Customer Covered Claims (Input IP infringement and usage violations) are included in the AI Addendum.

fields.name: ai_acceptable_use_policy
fields.description: Set to true when use of AI Services is subject to an Acceptable Use Policy at the specified ai_policy_reference.

fields.description: Whether the Provider signatory is an entity or individual

fields.description: Full legal name of the Provider's signatory

fields.description: Date this amended and restated certificate is EXECUTED, printed on the signature block ("executed by a duly authorized officer of this corporation on <date>") — supply the execution/signature date, not a later intended filing date. Under 8 Del. C. Section 103 the charter becomes legally effective on filing with the Delaware Secretary of State (or a specified delayed effective time), which may differ from this execution date. Accepts ISO YYYY-MM-DD (rendered as e.g. "July 15, 2026") or a display-ready date string.

items.name: openagreements-confidentiality-invention-assignment-agreement

items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Baseline structure inspired by permissive public references including the Balanced Employee IP Agreement (CC0) and Papertrail legal-docs (CC0). Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

items.name: openagreements-confidentiality-invention-assignment-agreement-nevada
items.description: Nevada-specific employee confidentiality and invention assignment agreement aligned with Nevada's employer-ownership default (NRS 600.500): the employer keeps statutory ownership of patentable inventions and trade secrets developed in the course and scope of employment that relate directly to the work, the own-time carve-out reaches only inventions outside that statutory grant, and the form carries no state carve-out notice because Nevada imposes none. The assignment clause keeps present-tense assignment language for a clean federal patent chain of title and carries no post-employment holdover tail. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Baseline structure inspired by permissive public references including the Balanced Employee IP Agreement (CC0) and Papertrail legal-docs (CC0). Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: prior_inventions_disclosure
fields.description: List of prior inventions excluded from assignment, or None

fields.name: excluded_inventions_statement
fields.description: Carveout statement for independently developed inventions
fields.default: Inventions Employee develops entirely on personal time without using company equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information are excluded, except for inventions that either (a) relate to company business or its actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development, or (b) result from work performed for the company. No exclusion covers any Employment-Scope IP.
fields.default_value_rationale: Nevada's NRS 600.500 makes the employer the sole owner, absent an express written agreement, of any patentable invention or trade secret developed in the course and scope of employment that relates directly to the work. The Employment-Scope IP defined term carries that statutory scope, and the final sentence keeps every exclusion outside it, so the form never gives away ownership the statute already vests in the employer.

items.name: openagreements-due-diligence-request-list
items.description: Modular legal due diligence request list (DDRL) for corporate transactions. Ships a base form covering organization, capitalization, contracts, IP, employment, litigation, compliance, tax, insurance, debt, property, and privacy/cybersecurity. Four optional industry riders gate via boolean toggles: tech / SaaS, life sciences, healthcare provider, and cross-border / trade compliance. Drafted clean-room from public-source consensus across 15 Am Law firm, bar association, and legal publisher DDRLs; no public source language is reused.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Category structure and consensus framing informed by publicly available DDRLs and commentary from Cooley, Skadden, Morgan Lewis, Gunderson Dettmer, Lowenstein Sandler, Holland & Knight, Foley & Lardner, Software Equity Group, the International Bar Association, the American Bar Association, Bass Berry & Sims, and Carta. See practice note for sources. Drafted clean-room; no source language was reused. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: requesting_firm_name
fields.description: Legal name of the buyer's law firm sending the request

fields.name: target_company_name
fields.description: Legal name of the target company receiving the request

fields.name: project_code_name
fields.description: Project code name or pseudonym used to refer to the transaction

fields.name: request_date
fields.description: Date the request list is delivered to the target

fields.name: response_due_date
fields.description: Date by which the target's first-round response is due

fields.name: lead_buyer_counsel_name
fields.description: Name of the lead attorney at the requesting firm

fields.name: lead_buyer_counsel_email
fields.description: Email of the lead attorney at the requesting firm

fields.name: primary_target_contact_name
fields.description: Name of the target's primary point of contact for diligence responses

fields.name: currency
fields.description: Currency for materiality and financial threshold inputs

fields.name: materiality_threshold_usd
fields.description: Dollar floor for material-contracts requests. Stored as a pre-formatted string (e.g., "$100,000") because the fill engine has no currency formatter. Adjust upward for larger deals, downward for early-stage targets.
fields.default_value_rationale: $100,000 is the consensus dollar floor for mid-market deals across public DDRLs (Cooley, Skadden, Bloomberg). Tune up for larger targets (e.g., "$1,000,000" for $100M+ deals) or down for early-stage.

fields.name: materiality_threshold_revenue_pct
fields.description: Relative materiality threshold expressed as a percentage of gross revenue. The lower of dollar floor and revenue percentage governs. Stored as a pre-formatted string for consistency with the dollar threshold.
fields.default_value_rationale: 5% of gross revenue is the consensus relative-materiality qualifier in BigLaw practice. Tune down for asset-light targets where individual contract size is small but each one is operationally critical.

fields.name: lookback_years
fields.description: Lookback window in years applied across litigation, compliance, and operational history. A few sections state their own fixed period in the prose (tax is 6 years; regulatory investigations, settlements, and consent decrees are open-ended).
fields.default_value_rationale: 3 years is consensus baseline for litigation, compliance, and operational history. Tax often warrants 6+ to track the IRS statute of limitations; regulatory correspondence is conventionally open-ended.

fields.name: tech_rider_enabled
fields.description: Enable Section P (Tech / SaaS rider) covering OSS schedules with copyleft flagging, source-code escrow, hosted-services agreements, SOC 2 / ISO 27001 audits, data-flow architecture, SLAs, security- incident history, and cloud-agreement change-of-control. Recommended on for software, SaaS, platform, marketplace, or data-heavy targets.

fields.name: life_sciences_rider_enabled
fields.description: Enable Section Q (Life Sciences rider) covering FDA correspondence, clinical trials, CRO agreements, Form 483s, Warning Letters, CIA, and controlled-substance registrations. Recommended on for biotech, pharma, medical-device, and diagnostics targets with FDA-regulated pipelines.

fields.name: healthcare_provider_rider_enabled
fields.description: Enable Section R (Healthcare Provider rider) covering HIPAA breach logs, billing/coding compliance, payer audits, malpractice claims, and Anti-Kickback / Stark / Sunshine Act compliance. Recommended on for clinics, hospitals, physician groups, and telehealth providers with payer-reimbursement exposure.

fields.name: cross_border_rider_enabled
fields.description: Enable Section S (Cross-Border / Trade Compliance rider) covering sanctions/OFAC screening, export-control classifications, AML/KYC, FCPA compliance, government-customer exposure, and foreign-investment- screening filings. Recommended on whenever the target has foreign subsidiaries, foreign customers, government customers, or sensitive- jurisdiction exposure. Increasingly mandatory after the 2023 DOJ M&A Safe Harbor Policy.

items.name: openagreements-employment-offer-letter

items.name: openagreements-founder-ip-confidentiality-confirmation
items.display_name: Founder IP and Confidentiality Survival Confirmation
items.description: A short confirmatory certificate signed by a departing founder and countersigned by the Delaware corporation confirming that the founder's existing Confidential Information and Invention Assignment Agreement (CIIAA/PIIA) and confidentiality obligations remain in full force after service ends, that all company property and repositories have been returned, and that no release is intended as to those continuing obligations. It is confirmatory only and creates no new restrictive covenant. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: company_legal_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the Delaware corporation

fields.name: founder_name
fields.description: Full name of the departing founder

fields.name: ciiaa_reference
fields.description: Reference to the founder's existing Confidential Information and Invention Assignment Agreement (for example, "Confidential Information and Invention Assignment Agreement dated January 1, 2022")

fields.name: separation_date
fields.description: Date the founder's service with the Company ended

fields.name: signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory countersigning for the Company

fields.name: signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory countersigning for the Company

items.name: openagreements-founder-separation-board-consent
items.description: Action by unanimous written consent of the board of directors of a Delaware corporation memorializing a founder's departure: accepting the resignation, removing the founder from officer titles, authorizing the repurchase of unvested shares under the founder's existing Restricted Stock Purchase Agreement, and managing the vacated board seat. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: company_legal_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the Delaware corporation

fields.name: consent_date
fields.description: Date the board consent is effective and signed

fields.name: founder_name
fields.description: Full name of the departing founder

fields.name: removed_offices
fields.description: Officer titles from which the founder is removed (for example, "Chief Executive Officer and Secretary")

fields.name: rspa_reference
fields.description: Reference to the founder's existing Restricted Stock Purchase Agreement (for example, "Restricted Stock Purchase Agreement dated January 1, 2022")

fields.name: vacancy_action
fields.description: How the board addresses the seat the founder vacated

fields.name: share_disposition
fields.description: The disposition of the repurchased shares resolved by the board — retired and restored to authorized-but-unissued, or held as treasury. Retirement of shares is a board act under DGCL Section 243(a), so the election belongs in this consent, not in an officer-level instruction.

fields.name: board_size_after
fields.description: Authorized number of directors after reduction (used when vacancy_action is reduce-board-size)

fields.name: replacement_director_name
fields.description: Full name of the director appointed to fill the vacancy (used when vacancy_action is appoint-replacement)

fields.name: director_signatories
fields.description: The directors signing the consent

items.name: name
items.description: Full name of a director signing the consent

items.name: openagreements-founder-separation-cap-table-update-instructions
items.description: Written instructions from an authorized officer of a Delaware corporation to the corporate secretary, transfer agent, or cap-table administrator to record a completed founder share repurchase: cancel or take possession of the repurchased certificates (or make the book-entry transfer for uncertificated shares), update the stock ledger, and reflect the disposition — cancelled and retired or held as treasury — on the cap table. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: company_legal_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the Delaware corporation

fields.name: founder_name
fields.description: Full name of the departing founder whose shares were repurchased

fields.name: certificate_numbers
fields.description: Certificate number(s) representing the repurchased shares (for example, "CS-12"), or a note that the shares were uncertificated

fields.name: shares_cancelled
fields.description: Number and class of repurchased shares to be recorded as cancelled or transferred (for example, "500,000 shares of Common Stock")

fields.name: cap_table_system
fields.description: The cap-table system or administrator to be updated (for example, "Carta")

fields.name: share_disposition
fields.description: How the repurchased shares are to be treated — cancelled and retired to authorized-but-unissued, or held as treasury shares

fields.name: signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized officer giving these instructions

fields.name: signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized officer giving these instructions

fields.name: instruction_date
fields.description: Date these instructions are given

items.name: openagreements-founder-separation-records-checklist
items.display_name: Founder Separation Records Certificate and Minute-Book Filing Checklist
items.description: A closing certificate and filing checklist for assembling a founder departure's executed instruments into a Delaware corporation's minute book: the board written consent, resignation letter, repurchase notice and repurchase agreement, proof of payment, any stockholder-consent notices, the IP/confidentiality confirmation, and the stock-ledger and cap-table updates. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: company_legal_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the Delaware corporation

fields.name: founder_name
fields.description: Full name of the departing founder

fields.name: minute_book_location
fields.description: Where the corporation's minute book is maintained (for example, "Carta" or "physical minute book at the Company's principal office")

fields.name: separation_date
fields.description: Effective date of the founder's separation

fields.name: certifying_officer_name
fields.description: Full name of the officer certifying the records have been filed

fields.name: certifying_officer_title
fields.description: Title of the certifying officer (for example, "Secretary" or "Chief Executive Officer")

items.name: openagreements-founder-separation-resignation-letter
items.description: A short letter from a departing founder to the board of directors of a Delaware corporation resigning from every officer and director position held, with an explicit effective date. The effective date is load-bearing: it fixes the termination date that starts the repurchase-option window under the founder's existing Restricted Stock Purchase Agreement. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: company_legal_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the Delaware corporation

fields.name: founder_name
fields.description: Full name of the departing founder

fields.name: resignation_effective_date
fields.description: The explicit effective date of the resignation. This date is load-bearing: it fixes the founder's termination date and starts the repurchase-option window under the founder's Restricted Stock Purchase Agreement.

fields.name: resigned_positions
fields.description: Every officer and director position from which the founder is resigning (for example, "Chief Executive Officer, Secretary, and member of the Board of Directors")

items.name: openagreements-founder-separation-stockholder-consent
items.description: Action by written consent of the stockholders of a Delaware corporation under Section 228 of the DGCL effecting a stockholder-reserved act in connection with a founder's departure — such as the forced removal of a non-resigning director under Section 141(k), an amendment to a charter that fixes board size, or the satisfaction of a voting agreement or protective provision. Used only when a stockholder act is required; a clean voluntary separation stops at the board consent. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: company_legal_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the Delaware corporation

fields.name: consent_date
fields.description: Date the stockholder consent is effective and signed

fields.name: founder_name
fields.description: Full name of the departing founder

fields.name: stockholder_action
fields.description: The stockholder-reserved act being effected by this consent (describe the act)

fields.name: removed_director_name
fields.description: Full name of the director being removed (used when stockholder_action is remove-director)

fields.name: stockholder_signatories
fields.description: The stockholders signing the consent

items.name: name
items.description: Full name of a stockholder signing the consent

items.name: shares
items.description: Number and class of shares held by the signing stockholder (for example, "1,000,000 shares of Common Stock")

items.name: openagreements-founder-share-repurchase-notice
items.description: Written notice from a Delaware corporation to a departing founder electing to exercise the company's pre-existing right to repurchase the founder's unvested shares within the exercise window set by the founder's Restricted Stock Purchase Agreement, identifying the governing agreement, termination date, unvested share count, per-share and aggregate price, and closing date. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.

fields.name: termination_date
fields.description: Date the founder's service terminated, which starts the RSPA exercise window

fields.name: repurchase_window_days

fields.name: unvested_share_count

fields.description: Per-share repurchase price set by the RSPA (for example, "$0.0001 per share")

fields.name: aggregate_price

fields.name: closing_date
fields.description: Date on which the repurchase closing will occur

fields.name: signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized officer signing for the company

fields.name: signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized officer signing for the company

items.name: openagreements-founder-stock-assignment-separate
items.description: A stock power (assignment separate from the certificate) by which a founder, for value received, assigns repurchased shares of a Delaware corporation back to the corporation and appoints an attorney-in-fact to transfer the shares on the corporation's books. Often pre-signed at issuance and held with the Restricted Stock Purchase Agreement or in escrow; here it effects the transfer of the repurchased shares. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-alabama
items.description: Alabama-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the Alabama Restrictive Covenant Act, Ala. Code section 8-1-190 et seq.: confidentiality and trade-secret protection, a current-customer non-solicitation, an employee no-hire covenant confined to uniquely essential workers, and an optional non-compete drawn to the section 8-1-190(b)(4) employee exception. Recites the void-by-default rule and its six safe-harbor categories, ties each covenant to a section 8-1-191 protectable interest, keeps durations within the statutory presumptions (two years employee non-compete, eighteen months current-customer non-solicit), confirms the section 8-1-192 all-parties signature and adequate-consideration formalities, avoids execution before the employment relationship exists, carves out the section 8-1-196 professional exemption, treats reformation as reaching only duration, and applies Alabama's fundamental-policy conflicts rule. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Alabama-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Alabama practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional). In Alabama the title is an early warning: a member of a recognized profession (physician, certified public accountant, veterinarian, physical therapist, or the like) cannot be barred from practicing under Alabama's professional exemption, so record the title and any licensure before including a practice-restraining covenant.

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement — the date the last party signs. It anchors the duration presumptions stated in Cover Terms.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. A finite term keeps the ordinary-confidentiality obligation from reading as an indefinite restraint on using general experience; job skills alone are not a protectable interest.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee no-hire / non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. The Act sets no express duration presumption for the no-hire category, so the uniquely-essential scope limit does the primary category work; counsel should size the term to the workforce interest actually protected.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the no-hire restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee no-hire scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of current-customer non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits well inside the presumptively reasonable window for a current-customer non-solicit: eighteen months, or for as long as post-separation consideration is paid, whichever is greater. Counsel may extend toward eighteen months, or longer while post-separation consideration is paid, where the interest supports it.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope. Alabama's category reaches only current customers, so the covered class is bounded to that group.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under Alabama law an employee may agree to a geographically limited non-compete; this template recites the employee-exception basis, ties the covenant to a listed protectable interest, and biases the duration into the two-year presumptively reasonable window.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Non-competes are the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include only after confirming the restraint fits Alabama's employee exception for a specified geographic area, a competing business, and an existing employment relationship; protects a listed interest; stays within the two-year presumption; will be signed by all parties, including the employer; and does not restrain a member of a recognized profession from practicing that profession. The party seeking enforcement bears the burden of proof on every element.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits well inside the presumptively reasonable window for an employee non-compete; restraints of two years or less are presumed reasonable. A term outside the window forfeits the statutory presumption and invites duration reformation, so the tool defaults short.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction. Alabama's employee exception is written for a geographically limited covenant, so a defined territory is part of fitting the statutory category, not an afterthought.
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employer carries on the business Employee served
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where Employer actually does competing business. Alabama's employee exception presupposes a geographically limited covenant; an unbounded or everywhere-we-might-expand territory undercuts the claim that the covenant fits the employee non-compete category at all.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Alabama rewards specificity: the protectable-interest list speaks in terms of specific customers and concrete relationships, and a covenant drawn to named threats is easier to defend as protecting a listed interest than one leaning on an open-ended competitive-business definition.

fields.name: specialized_training_recital
fields.description: Alabama only. Where specialized training is a claimed protectable interest, Alabama law requires the specialized and unique training, involving substantial business expenditure specifically directed to this Employee, to be specifically set forth in writing as the consideration for the restraint. State that training and its anticipated expense specifically here; a generic training recital does not qualify. Leave blank if the covenants rest on trade secrets, confidential information, customer relationships, or goodwill instead.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits within the current-customer window. Because non-dealing has no category of its own, it is drawn to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception, and its duration tracks that category's presumption.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). In a state whose law voids restraints except as it specifically provides, a clause forbidding ordinary public shares is gratuitous overbreadth serving no listed interest.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months keeps the restraint conservative. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category, so it is drawn to fit a listed exception or accepted as exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories; a short term minimizes that exposure.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-alaska
items.description: Alaska-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Alaska's pure common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Alaska non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on Alaska Supreme Court case law — non-competition agreements are strictly construed and weighed against the common-law reasonableness factors (reasonably necessary to protect a legitimate business interest and no broader than needed). It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection alongside the Alaska Uniform Trade Secrets Act (AS 45.50.910–.945), customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional customer non-dealing covenant scoped to the customers and information the employer procured at its own expense, an optional non-compete drawn to the reasonable-necessity gate, optional non-investment, sale-of-business public-injury weighing for covenants tied to a business sale, and a reasonable-alteration posture that the drafting does not rely on — judicial rescue of an overbroad covenant is available only to an employer that carries the burden of proving good-faith drafting, with willful overreach forfeiting alteration. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Alaska-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Alaska practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: covenant_relationship_type
fields.description: Whether the covenants arise from ordinary employment or from the sale of a business. A sale-of-business covenant is weighed under the Wenzell balance (purchased goodwill against seller hardship and likely injury to the public), not the ordinary employment analysis.
fields.default_value_rationale: Most restrictive covenants in this template arise from ordinary employment. Set to sale_of_business only where the restraint is tied to a business sale, which triggers the Wenzell public-injury weighing in the Sale-of-Business Covenants section.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Alaska sets no statutory term; counsel should size it to the actual sensitivity of the information.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Alaska sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the fact-bound Data Management reasonableness factors rather than a fixed number. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Alaska sets no statutory cap. Metcalfe upheld a narrow customer-list restraint even without a stated durational limit, but a stated period is easier to defend; 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Alaska has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it is reasonably necessary to protect a legitimate business interest and no broader than needed under the Data Management reasonableness factors, and it is strictly construed. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant — Alaska strictly construes non-competition agreements and a restraint aimed at ordinary competition or the worker's livelihood fails the factors no matter how modest its time and territory look. A customer non-solicit backstopped by the Alaska Uniform Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified protectable interest (confidential information, trade secrets, or customer goodwill) and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Alaska imposes no statutory cap on duration; time limits are one Data Management factor among many, weighed together with territory against the employer's real protectable interest, so there is no safe-harbor number. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Alaska imposes no statutory cap on territory and a statewide covenant is not automatically invalid, but every mile must map to the business interest at stake; a narrower area genuinely needed to protect customer relationships or confidential information is far easier to defend as good-faith drafting than an unexplained statewide ban.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives).

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-arizona
items.description: Arizona-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Arizona's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Arizona non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on Arizona's reasonableness test — a restriction is unreasonable and will not be enforced if it is greater than necessary to protect the employer's legitimate interest, or if that interest is outweighed by hardship to the employee and likely injury to the public. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the Arizona Uniform Trade Secrets Act (A.R.S. §§ 44-401 to 44-407), a continued-at-will-employment consideration recital, customer and employee non-solicitation drawn to the protectable-interest threshold, an optional non-compete sized to the actual footprint, confidentiality and non-solicits scoped so they do not operate as de-facto non-competes, a broadcast-employer screening recital for the one statutory ban (A.R.S. § 23-494), physician covenants drafted for Arizona's heightened public-interest scrutiny, and grammatically severable covenant drafting for Arizona's strict trim-only blue pencil, which strikes overbroad terms but never rewrites them. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Arizona-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Arizona practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: Arizona sets no statutory cap, but an unlimited confidentiality covenant reaching non-secret information has been held unenforceable as the equivalent of a geographically unrestricted non-compete, so ordinary confidential information carries a finite term. 24 months is a common, conservative duration for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations that counsel should size to the information actually protected.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Arizona sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the common-law reasonableness test and, because anti-piracy covenants face the same no-broader-than-necessary test as non-competes, on the employer's actual protectable interest. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, sized to the workforce relationships actually protected.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin. Arizona sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps onto the customer-goodwill interest but is enforceable only as far as the employer's protectable interest in the specific relationships reaches, so counsel should size the term to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Arizona has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it satisfies the Farber reasonableness test and is tied to a legitimate protectable interest rather than ordinary competition. Because Arizona's blue pencil is strictly trim-only, an overbroad covenant is struck rather than rewritten, so this template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint from the start.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under the Farber test, and Arizona disfavors restraints on pursuing a similar vocation. A customer non-solicit backstopped by the Arizona Uniform Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified protectable interest (confidential information, trade secrets, or customer goodwill) and sizing the duration, territory, and activity scope to the actual footprint, because no Arizona court will pare an overbroad covenant back to a lawful size.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a common, conservative pick rather than an Arizona ceiling (Arizona imposes no statutory cap on duration). Courts weigh time, territory, and activity together against the employer's real protectable interest under Farber, so there is no safe-harbor number; counsel should size the default to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form, mindful that the strict blue pencil means an unreasonable duration is struck, not shortened.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Arizona imposes no statutory cap on territory; a narrower area genuinely needed to protect customer relationships or confidential information is far easier to defend under Farber than a statewide ban, and because an Arizona court strikes an overbroad territory rather than shrinking it, the map must match the protectable interest from the start.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-arkansas
items.description: Arkansas-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Ark. Code Ann. section 4-75-101: confidentiality and trade-secret protection, employee and customer non-solicitation, a non-dealing covenant, and an optional non-compete drawn to the statutory protectable-interest and time-and-scope test. Recites the statutory categories of protectable business interest, keeps durations within the two-year presumptively-reasonable window, relies on the continued-employment consideration rule, voids any practice restriction on a physician under the 2025 amendment, flags the Title 17, Subtitle 3 licensed-professional exclusion, and acknowledges the mandatory judicial reformation remedy. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Arkansas-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Arkansas practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement — the date the last party signs. It anchors the durations stated in Cover Terms.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Arkansas keeps confidentiality agreements on a different track from covenants about competition or competitive work, so a finite term on non-secret information is a common-law reasonableness choice.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Arkansas places agreements not to solicit, recruit, or hire employees outside the non-compete statute when they do not concern competition or competitive work, so this covenant is judged under common-law reasonableness and cannot borrow the statutory reformation or consideration rules.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits within the two-year presumptively reasonable window. A customer non-solicit that concerns competitive work may be tested under the statutory framework, so its duration is kept inside the presumption.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope, and a specific customer group tied to the employee's contacts is an Arkansas reasonableness factor for tailoring a customer-facing restraint.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Arkansas tests a non-compete by whether it is ancillary to the employment relationship, backed by a protectable business interest, and limited in time and scope no greater than necessary to defend that interest; this template recites the protectable-interest basis and biases the duration into the two-year presumptively reasonable window.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Non-competes are the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include only after confirming a real protectable business interest, that the duration is within the two-year presumption, that the worker is not a physician whose right to practice within scope would be restricted, and that the worker does not hold a Title 17, Subtitle 3 professional license that takes the covenant outside the statute.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits well inside the two-year presumptively reasonable window. Arkansas authority has recited the historical pattern that one- and two-year limits were upheld while three- and five-year limits were unreasonable, so the tool defaults short.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction. Arkansas law does not treat a missing geographic restriction as automatically overbroad if time and scope remain no greater than necessary; where geography is stated, tie it to where the protected interest is actually at risk.
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services or had material customer contact
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service and contact area. A defined territory should track where the protected interest is at risk; where no geography is stated, Arkansas law shifts the limiting burden onto the customer and activity scope, which this template supplies.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Limiting the restriction to a specific group of entities associated with the employer's business is an Arkansas reasonableness factor and strengthens enforceability.
fields.default_value_rationale: Empty by default because named-competitor limits are deal-specific. When supplied, naming a specific competitor group can strengthen tailoring by limiting the restraint to entities connected to the employer's business.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits within the two-year presumption. Non-dealing restrains competitive work even when the customer calls first, so it is drawn to the statutory tailoring test.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A clause that forbids ordinary public shares restrains conduct that threatens no protectable interest, exactly the excess width an Arkansas court would trim.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits within the two-year presumption. An active-ownership investment restraint functions as a non-compete and is drawn to the same statutory tailoring test.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-california
items.description: California-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement centered on confidentiality, trade secret protection, property return, and non-disparagement. Omits employee competition, customer-solicitation, employee-solicitation, non-dealing, and non-investment employment restraints under Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code sections 16600, 16600.1, and 16600.5, and includes California choice-of-law and forum language reflecting Cal. Lab. Code section 925. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
