items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-colorado
items.description: Colorado-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for C.R.S. § 8-2-113: confidentiality and trade-secret protection with the statutory general-skills and protected-disclosure carve-outs, an employee non-solicitation covenant, a customer non-solicitation covenant gated on sixty percent of the highly compensated worker threshold ($78,008.40 for 2026), and an optional non-compete gated on the highly compensated worker threshold ($130,014 for 2026) and tied to trade-secret protection. Both earnings gates run at signing and at enforcement. The agreement flags the separate, signed 14-day advance-notice document the statute requires, excludes restrictive covenants for health-care providers under S.B. 25-083 with the patient-communication protections, keeps Colorado law and a Colorado forum for Colorado-based workers, and drafts each covenant within its enforceable scope rather than relying on discretionary judicial reformation. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Colorado-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Colorado practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement — the date the last party signs. It anchors the durations and the compensation-threshold test in Cover Terms.

fields.name: notice_date
fields.description: Date the employer delivered the separate, signed advance-notice document required by the Colorado Noncompetition Agreement Act. For a prospective worker the notice must precede acceptance of the offer; for a current worker it must be delivered at least 14 days before the earlier of the covenant's effective date or the effective date of the consideration for the covenant.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For a worker who primarily resided and worked in Colorado at termination, Colorado law governs enforceability of the covenants notwithstanding any contrary clause, so this should remain Colorado for a Colorado-based worker.

fields.name: highly_compensated_threshold
fields.description: Annualized cash compensation figure that gates the non-compete. The Division of Labor Standards and Statistics resets the highly compensated worker threshold annually through the PAY CALC Order; the controlling figure is the greater of the August 10, 2022 amount or the amount in effect when the covenant is executed. The default is the 2026 figure; replace it with the figure for the execution year.
fields.default_value_rationale: $130,014 is the 2026 highly compensated worker threshold set by the 2026 PAY CALC Order. The Division resets it annually, so counsel should confirm the figure for the covenant's execution year.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_threshold
fields.description: Annualized cash compensation figure that gates the customer non-solicit, equal to sixty percent of the highly compensated worker threshold. The default is the 2026 derived figure; replace it with sixty percent of the threshold for the execution year.
fields.default_value_rationale: $78,008.40 is sixty percent of the 2026 highly compensated worker threshold of $130,014. The PAY CALC Order does not state this figure, so confirm the current-year derived amount before using the covenant.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Colorado builds a reasonableness requirement into its confidentiality carve-out, so a finite term keeps the non-secret obligation within the safe harbor.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Colorado does not statutorily address employee non-solicits, and case law treats a clause limited to active solicitation as a lesser restraint, so counsel preferring to underline that characterization can shorten the term.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. A Colorado customer non-solicit must be no broader than reasonably necessary to protect trade secrets, and a shorter term reduces the room for the enforcement-time earnings test to fall out from under it.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under the Colorado Noncompetition Agreement Act a Colorado covenant not to compete is void by default; it survives only under the highly compensated worker exception, which requires the worker to earn at or above the threshold both when the covenant is entered into and when it is enforced and requires the covenant to protect trade secrets and be no broader than reasonably necessary. This template recites those gates when the covenant is included.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. A Colorado non-compete is void unless the employer can fit it into the narrow highly compensated worker exception, and merely presenting a void covenant is unlawful, carrying actual damages plus a $5,000-per-worker penalty. Include only after confirming the worker clears the threshold at signing and enforcement, the covenant protects an identified trade-secret interest, the separate signed notice was delivered, and the worker is not a health-care provider barred by the 2025 health-care provider amendments.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. Because the worker must still meet the earnings threshold at the time the covenant is enforced, a longer period widens the window for the enforcement-time facts to defeat the covenant.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which the protected trade secrets would be exploited
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where the protected trade secrets would actually be exploited rather than the employer's footprint. Colorado's exception extends only as far as reasonably necessary to protect trade secrets, and an overbroad territory should not be drafted on the assumption that a court will narrow it.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming the employer's real competitors is strong evidence the covenant is no broader than reasonably necessary, which matters in Colorado because a court will not rewrite an overbroad clause into a lawful one.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives).

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: healthcare_provider
fields.description: Whether Employee is a healthcare provider whose covenant implicates profession-specific restrictions, patient access, or continuity-of-care requirements. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role; do not infer it from the agreement's jurisdiction.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-connecticut
items.description: Connecticut-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Connecticut's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Connecticut non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the five-factor common-law reasonableness test (partial and restricted as to time or place, fair protection of the employer, no undue hardship on the employee, no interference with the public interest, and no preclusion of the employee's occupation and livelihood). It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Conn. Gen. Stat. §§ 35-51 to 35-53), a continued-employment consideration recital drafted to prove the connection Connecticut's current consideration case law requires, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete tied to a recognized protectable interest and sized to the employer's actual footprint, a physician clause reciting the § 20-14p one-year and fifteen-mile caps and the parallel §§ 20-12k / 20-101d caps for physician assistants and APRNs, statutory carve-outs excluding security-guard (§ 31-50a), broadcast-employee (§ 31-50b), and homemaker-companion / home-health (§§ 20-681, 20-683) restraints, and separable, tiered restraints drafted within their enforceable scope rather than in reliance on discretionary judicial narrowing, which Connecticut authority leaves unsettled. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Connecticut-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Connecticut practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position. In Connecticut the worker's occupation selects the rulebook — physicians, physician assistants, APRNs, security guards, broadcast employees, and home-care workers each sit under a different statutory rule, and everyone else gets common-law balancing — so record the actual title and duties.

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. The execution date does heavy lifting for clinician covenants: the statutory caps reach covenants entered into, amended, extended, or renewed on or after July 1, 2016 for physicians and October 1, 2023 for physician assistants and APRNs.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For a Connecticut workforce the honest selection is Connecticut law and a Connecticut forum, because the covenant was drafted against the five-factor common-law reasonableness test and Connecticut's occupation statutes; a foreign-law clause invites a choice-of-law fight on top of the merits.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Connecticut's reasonableness test supports protecting confidential information for a reasonable period of time, not forever, so a finite term keeps the non-secret obligation within that reasonableness lens.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Connecticut sets no statutory cap on duration; no occupation statute addresses employee non-solicits, so the clause is judged on the ordinary five-factor common-law reasonableness test. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Connecticut sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit limited to customers the worker serviced or solicited is the shape Connecticut courts have rewarded, and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Connecticut has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it satisfies the five-factor Connecticut reasonableness test and is tied to a recognized protectable interest rather than ordinary competition. Occupation statutes cap or bar the covenant for certain workers before any balancing begins. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under Connecticut's five-factor test, and several Connecticut occupation statutes cap it for physicians, physician assistants, and APRNs or bar it outright for security guards absent proven trade secrets, broadcast employees, and homemaker-companion / home-health workers. A customer non-solicit tied to serviced-or-solicited customers often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming the worker's occupation clears the statutory gates and after identifying a recognized protectable interest — trade secrets, confidential information, customer lists, or customer goodwill — and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Connecticut imposes no general statutory cap on duration for ordinary employees — courts have upheld even a five-year restriction on the right record — but the period must be justified by the record and is weighed together with geography under the five-factor test, so there is no safe-harbor number. Physician, physician assistant, and APRN covenants are separately capped at one year. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which the employer actually does business and Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where the employer actually does business and the employee served, because a covenant that protects the employer in areas where it does not and is unlikely to do business is unreasonable as to area. Statewide can be defensible when the customer footprint is statewide, but aspiration is not a footprint, and physician, physician assistant, and APRN covenants are separately capped at fifteen miles from the primary practice site.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming the employer's real competitors is self-proving narrowness under the fair-protection factor and leaves the worker free to take the rest of the industry's jobs, which defuses the livelihood objection under the reasonableness test.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A clause that technically forbids holding index funds or ordinary public shares adds harshness the employer cannot justify under the fair-protection factor of the reasonableness test.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-delaware
items.description: Delaware-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Delaware's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Delaware non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the Court of Chancery's three-part review (reasonable in geographic scope and temporal duration, advancing a legitimate economic interest, and surviving a balancing of the equities), evaluated holistically across every dimension of the covenant. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (6 Del. C. §§ 2001-2007), a formation-stage consideration recital that names the value exchanged, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete sized to identified legitimate interests, physician practice-covenant compliance that voids any practice restraint while preserving reasonably related damages (6 Del. C. § 2707), a home-inspector-trainee carve-out (24 Del. C. § 4109(d)), a Delaware choice-of-law anchor bounded by its statutory and conflict-of-law limits (6 Del. C. § 2708), and a draft-the-scope reformation posture the agreement does not rely on because recent decisions increasingly refuse to blue-pencil an overbroad covenant. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Delaware-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Delaware practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Delaware sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the Court of Chancery's holistic reasonableness review rather than a fixed number. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual legitimate interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Delaware sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit tied to specific, identified customer relationships is the business-specific interest Delaware credits and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Delaware has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it satisfies the Court of Chancery's three-part reasonableness review — it must be reasonable in geographic scope and temporal duration, advance a legitimate economic interest, and survive a balancing of the equities — evaluated holistically against every other restriction in the contract. This template recites the legitimate-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint at the drafting table, because recent decisions increasingly refuse to blue-pencil an overbroad covenant.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under the Delaware reasonableness review; a customer non-solicit backstopped by the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming a recognized legitimate interest (employer goodwill or confidential information) and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint — a court may let an overbroad covenant fail rather than rewrite it.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Delaware imposes no statutory cap on duration; the Court of Chancery weighs time and territory together with every other dimension against the employer's real legitimate interest, so there is no safe-harbor number. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Geographic scope is the first prong of the Delaware reasonableness review; a narrower area genuinely needed to protect employer goodwill or confidential information is far easier to defend than a broad or nationwide ban, and an overbroad territory risks the whole covenant because Delaware courts increasingly decline to trim overreach.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives).

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-district-of-columbia
items.description: District of Columbia employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the Ban on Non-Compete Agreements Amendment Act of 2020 (D.C. Code §§ 32-581.01–.04b): confidentiality and trade-secret protection with the statutory information carve-out, an employee non-solicitation covenant, a customer non-solicitation covenant, and an optional non-compete confined to a highly compensated employee — one at or above the annually indexed minimum qualifying annual compensation ($162,164 for most employees in 2026; $270,274 for a medical specialist). The non-compete recites every § 32-581.03(a) validity gate: a stated functional scope and geographic limits, a term of no more than 365 calendar days from separation (730 for a medical specialist), and delivery of the covenant in writing at least 14 days in advance, with the prescribed statutory notice and the workplace-policy disclosure flagged as companion writings. For a covered employee below the threshold the covenant is banned and void as a matter of law, so the template omits it by default and never relies on judicial reformation to cure overbreadth. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. District of Columbia-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified District of Columbia practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement — the date the last party signs. It anchors the durations stated in Cover Terms.

fields.name: notice_date
fields.description: Date the employer delivered the non-compete provision to the employee in writing. For a valid highly compensated employee non-compete the employer must provide it at least 14 days before the individual commences employment, or, for a current employee, at least 14 days before the employee must execute the agreement.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law jurisdiction. Coverage under the District's ban keys to where the employee works — more than 50% of work time in the District, or a District-based role with substantial District work — not to the law named in the clause, so this should remain the District of Columbia for a District-based worker.

fields.name: minimum_qualifying_compensation
fields.description: Minimum qualifying annual compensation the worker must earn to be a highly compensated employee who may be bound by a non-compete. The statutory base is $150,000, adjusted upward each year for inflation; the Department of Employment Services publishes the current figure. The default is the 2026 figure; replace it with the figure for the year the covenant is proposed.
fields.default_value_rationale: $162,164 is the 2026 minimum qualifying annual compensation published in the D.C. Department of Employment Services Public Notice on the Prohibition on Non-Compete Clauses (Jan. 1, 2026). The statute indexes this figure to inflation each year, so counsel should confirm the published figure for the year the covenant is proposed rather than relying on the $150,000 statutory base.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. A finite term keeps the non-secret obligation from functioning as a bar on competitive employment, which would risk recharacterization as a banned non-compete provision.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. The District's ban does not speak to employee non-solicits, so the clause is judged under common-law reasonableness; a shorter term supports treating it as a limited restraint on active solicitation.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. A District customer non-solicit sits outside the statutory ban but is still a restraint of trade judged for reasonableness under common law (Ellis), and a shorter term supports that characterization.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Beginning October 1, 2022 an employer may not require or request that a covered employee — a District-based worker below the minimum qualifying annual compensation — sign or comply with a non-compete provision, and such a provision entered into on or after that date is void as a matter of law. A non-compete survives only against a highly compensated employee and only if it clears every statutory validity gate: a stated functional scope and geographic limits, a term of no more than 365 calendar days from separation (730 for a medical specialist), and 14-day advance written delivery. This template recites those gates when the covenant is included.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. For a covered employee a District non-compete is banned and void, and subjecting an employee to one carries monetary relief of $500–$1,000 per employee, at least $1,500 for an attempt to enforce a void covenant, and at least $3,000 for a subsequent violation, plus administrative penalties and Attorney General enforcement. Include only after confirming the worker is a highly compensated employee at or above the published threshold, the covenant states its functional scope and geography, its term stays within the calendar-day cap, and the covenant was delivered in writing at least 14 days in advance with the prescribed statutory notice.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of the non-competition restriction, measured in calendar days from separation. It must not exceed 365 calendar days from the date the employee separates, or 730 calendar days for a medical specialist.
fields.default_value_rationale: 365 calendar days is the statutory ceiling for a non-medical highly compensated employee measured from separation. A term stated in years risks silently overshooting the day count, so the default is expressed in calendar days; a medical specialist may run to 730 calendar days.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction. Section 32-581.03(a)(1)(B) makes stated geographical limitations a condition of a valid highly compensated employee non-compete, so this must be an explicit, bounded territory matched to where the employee works.
fields.default: the geographic area in which the Employee performed work for Employer
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where the employee actually performed work rather than the employer's entire footprint. Section 32-581.03(a)(1)(B) requires the covenant to specify its geographical limitations, and a covenant silent on geography is not valid and enforceable even for a worker far above the pay threshold.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the services, roles, industry, or competing entities the employee is restricted from performing work in or on behalf of. Section 32-581.03(a)(1)(A) makes a stated functional scope a condition of a valid highly compensated employee non-compete.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competing entities. Naming the employer's real competitors is the most concrete way to satisfy the competing-entities limb of the functional-scope requirement and shrinks the scope a reviewer must defend.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-florida
items.description: Florida-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement with modular covenant structure (non-compete, non-solicitation, non-dealing, non-investment, confidentiality, non-disparagement). Implements Fla. Stat. section 542.335 (legitimate-business-interest recital, mandatory judicial modification of overbroad restraints, express assignee / successor / third-party-beneficiary enforcement), the section 542.336 physician specialty-monopoly carve-out, and the 2025 CHOICE Act (sections 542.41-542.45) covered-employee enhanced- enforcement track. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Florida-specific analysis informed by publicly available legal commentary cited in the related practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: employer_parent_name
fields.description: Legal name of Employer's direct corporate parent entity, if any (optional). Named alongside "Employer and its Affiliates" as an expressly-identified third-party beneficiary entitled to enforce the restrictive covenants under Florida's restrictive-covenant rules. Common in PE / holding-company structures where the named Employer entity is a dedicated employment entity distinct from the operating company.
fields.default_value_rationale: Blank is the conservative default because many employers do not have a separate parent entity that should be named individually. Add the parent only when a real parent beneficiary should be expressly identified.

fields.name: employer_operating_affiliate_name
fields.description: Legal name of the operating or service-recipient Affiliate, if different from the named Employer (optional). Use this where the named Employer is a dedicated employment entity but the operating business — the entity for whom Employee actually performs services or that holds the Protected Interests — is a separate commonly controlled Affiliate (common in PE / holding-company structures). When provided, this entity is expressly named in the third-party-beneficiary list, in addition to the "Employer and its Affiliates" class language, for higher enforcement certainty.
fields.default_value_rationale: Blank is the conservative default because the named Employer is often also the operating business. Add a separate operating affiliate only when the service recipient or owner of the Protected Interests is a distinct commonly controlled entity.

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: garden_leave_notice_duration
fields.description: Length of the covered garden leave notice period (e.g. "6 months", "1 year"). A covered garden leave agreement may provide for up to, but no more than, 4 years of advance notice; after the first 90 days of the notice period the employee need not provide services. Shown in Cover Terms and recited in the garden-leave clause when garden_leave_included is true. The non-compete Restricted Period is reduced day-for-day by the nonworking portion of this period.
fields.default_value_rationale: 6 months is a moderate notice period well within the 4-year statutory ceiling. The employer should set this to the actual negotiated notice period; it must not exceed 4 years.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. For former-employee covenants, 24 months does not exceed the 2-year presumptively-unreasonable threshold, though it sits above the 6-month presumptively-reasonable window.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin. It also remains below the 2-year presumptively unreasonable threshold for former-employee covenants, although it is above the 6-month presumptively reasonable threshold.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under Florida's restrictive-covenant rules, a non-compete is enforceable when supported by a legitimate business interest and reasonable in time, area, and line of business; this template recites the legitimate-business-interest basis and biases the duration away from the presumptively unreasonable range.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Non-competes are the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include only after confirming a legitimate business interest exists, the duration is not in the presumptively unreasonable range, and (for a Physician) the specialty-monopoly carve-out is addressed.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a common, conservative pick that also remains below the 2-year presumptively unreasonable threshold for a former-employee non-compete, although it is above the 6-month presumptively reasonable threshold. The tool should not default to a presumptively-unreasonable term.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Narrower geographic scope is more readily found reasonable; Florida courts will modify rather than void an overbroad territory.

fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-georgia
items.description: Georgia-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the Georgia Restrictive Covenants Act (O.C.G.A. § 13-8-50 et seq.): confidentiality and trade-secret protection keyed to continued protection under § 13-8-53(e), a customer non-solicitation anchored to material-contact customers under § 13-8-53(b) with no express geographic term required, an optional non-compete gated on the § 13-8-53(a) covered-employee functions and drafted to reasonable time, geographic area, and scope within the § 13-8-57(b) two-year presumption window, discretionary narrowing-only judicial modification under § 13-8-53(d), and Georgia choice-of-law language reflecting that Georgia law is the touchstone for enforceability. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Georgia-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Georgia practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: covered_employee_function
fields.description: Description of the covered-employee function under the Georgia Restrictive Covenants Act that qualifies Employee to be bound by a post-employment non-compete (customarily soliciting customers, making sales, the enumerated managerial duties, or serving as a key employee or professional). Recorded so the covenant documents that Employee falls within a covered category.
fields.default: Employee is a key employee or a professional, or in the course of employment customarily and regularly solicits customers or prospective customers, makes sales, or performs the managerial duties described in O.C.G.A. § 13-8-53(a).
fields.default_value_rationale: The default recites the covered employee categories used by the Georgia Restrictive Covenants Act so the non-compete gate is documented before the optional non-compete is included.

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement — the date the last party signs. It anchors the durations stated in Cover Terms.

fields.name: additional_consideration
fields.description: Consideration provided to Employee for entering into this agreement. The Georgia Restrictive Covenants Act does not codify what consideration must support a covenant, and no modern Act-era appellate decision squarely holds that continued at-will employment alone supports a mid-employment covenant, so for an existing employee reciting additional consideration (a raise, bonus, promotion, or access to Confidential Information) is the conservative practice.
fields.default: access to Employer's Confidential Information, specialized training, and Employer's customer goodwill and relationships

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets
fields.default: For as long as the information remains a trade secret
fields.default_value_rationale: Georgia law places no fixed time limit on trade-secret protection for as long as the material remains a trade secret, so a fixed expiry would unnecessarily surrender the most durable protection.

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default: For as long as the information remains confidential
fields.default_value_rationale: Georgia law authorizes an open-ended confidentiality obligation keyed to continued confidentiality; a stated finite term for ordinary business information remains an acceptable alternative.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation (non-recruitment) restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Employee non-recruitment covenants are analyzed under the Georgia restrictive-covenant framework, so 24 months tracks the two-year former-employee presumption window.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: Bounding the no-poach class to colleagues the departing worker actually worked with or supervised during a stated look-back window is easier to defend under the Act's reasonableness standard than a payroll-wide ban; 24 months aligns the lookback with the two-year employee covenant period.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months sits within the two-year presumptively reasonable window measured from termination of the business relationship.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers count as material-contact customers under the Georgia Restrictive Covenants Act.
fields.default_value_rationale: Georgia law defines material contact to include customers from whom the employee earned compensation within two years before termination, so the lookback tracks the statutory period.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under the Georgia Restrictive Covenants Act, a post-employment non-compete is enforceable only against an employee who performs a covered function (customarily soliciting customers, making sales, the enumerated managerial duties, or serving as a key employee or professional) and only if the restraint is reasonable in time, geographic area, and scope of prohibited activities. This template recites the covered-function basis and biases the duration into the two-year presumption window.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily gated covenant in Georgia; include only after confirming Employee performs a covered function, the duration is within the two-year window, and Employer can plead and prove a legitimate business interest.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: A former-employee restraint of two years or less is presumed reasonable in time and a restraint over two years is presumed unreasonable, so the tool should not default beyond 24 months.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction. Optional under Georgia law: an express geographic term is not required for every enforceable non-compete.
fields.default: the geographic areas in which Employer conducts business
fields.default_value_rationale: An express territory tracking where the employer does business earns the presumption of geographic reasonableness when the total distance is reasonable or the agreement names particular competitors; leaving it blank may be permitted but forgoes that presumption.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming competitors for a limited period is one route by which an employer's-business-areas territory earns the presumption of geographic reasonableness.
fields.default_value_rationale: No default competitor list is supplied because competitor names depend on the employer's actual market and should be filled only when the employer can identify a limited, accurate list.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives) and keeps ordinary public shares and index funds out of the scope of prohibited activities the Act weighs for reasonableness.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-hawaii
items.description: Hawaii-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for HRS chapter 480's restraint-of-trade framework: confidentiality and trade-secret protection, customer and employee non-solicitation, and an optional non-compete, each drawn to the legitimate-ancillary-purpose screen of HRS section 480-4(c) and the statutory reasonableness factors. Non-competes and employee non-solicits are struck automatically when the employer is a technology business (HRS section 480-4(d)), leaving the trade-secret covenant as the surviving restraint. The Solicit definition tracks the active-initiation standard, and each restraint is drawn to its minimum supporting interest rather than in reliance on judicial narrowing. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Hawaii-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Hawaii practice note (HRS sections 480-4, 480-13, 480-2, 482B-8; Prudential Locations, LLC v. Gagnon; 7's Enterprises, Inc. v. Del Rosario; Technicolor, Inc. v. Traeger). Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is a common finite term for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Hawaii sets no statutory ceiling; a finite term keeps the practical restraint auditable under Hawaii's anticompetitive-effect screen.

fields.name: employer_is_technology_business
fields.description: Hawaii only. Whether the employer is a technology business: a trade or business that derives the majority of its gross income from selling or licensing products or services resulting from its software development or information technology development, or both, excluding broadcast businesses and certain regulated telecommunications carriers. When true, the non-competition and employee non-solicitation covenants are prohibited, void, and of no force and effect against an employee of the technology business, and only the trade-secret covenant survives.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Coverage keys to the EMPLOYER'S revenue mix, not the worker's job title; confirm whether the employing entity derives the majority of its gross income from software or information technology development before including any non-compete or employee non-solicit.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Hawaii fixes no statutory maximum. Length of time is one of the factors a court examines under the Traeger reasonableness test; the period should be the shortest the workforce-stability interest credibly requires. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; it is offered as a market reference, not a statutory benchmark, and counsel should shorten toward 12 months or less where the interest is exhausted sooner.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common lookback period. It keeps the covered class tethered to colleagues the departing worker actually worked with, which supports the legitimate ancillary purpose the covenant needs under Hawaii law.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Hawaii fixes no statutory maximum; duration is weighed for reasonableness under the Traeger factors. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative starting point tied to the near-permanence of the customer relationship the covenant protects, for counsel to shorten or justify.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common lookback period, keeping the covered class limited to special customer relationships the worker actually built — an interest Hawaii recognizes as protectable (Del Rosario).

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. In Hawaii, a non-compete is a restraint of trade lawful only when ancillary to a legitimate purpose and not substantially anticompetitive; preventing competition is not a legitimate purpose. Even a covenant that satisfies every Traeger reasonableness factor is unenforceable without that ancillary purpose, and it is void entirely for an employee of a technology business.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant in Hawaii; include only after confirming the employer is not a technology business, that a specific protected interest (trade secrets, genuinely confidential information, special customer relationships, or specialized training combined with other factors) supports it, and that its scope is drawn no broader than that interest requires.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Hawaii imposes no statutory maximum duration, but length of time is one of the Traeger reasonableness factors and a reasonable period is a condition of the statutory covenant framework. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative starting point for counsel to shorten or justify against the specific protected interest, not a statutory safe harbor.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Geographic scope is examined alongside duration and breadth, and the contract's own territory controls what can be enforced — in Del Rosario a statewide injunction was remanded to the covenant's County of Honolulu limit. Tie the territory to where the protected interest actually operates.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Alabama rewards specificity: the protectable-interest list speaks in terms of specific customers and concrete relationships, and a covenant drawn to named threats is easier to defend as protecting a listed interest than one leaning on an open-ended competitive-business definition.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a conservative starting point; duration is weighed under the Traeger factors and should be justified against the specific customer relationship the covenant protects.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives), and a carve-out avoids adding undue hardship — one of the three Traeger grounds of unreasonableness — with no protected interest behind it.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a conservative starting point; an investment restraint is a restraint of trade weighed under the same ancillary-purpose and Traeger reasonableness limits as the non-compete.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-idaho
items.description: Idaho-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Idaho Code title 44, chapter 27: confidentiality and trade-secret protection, customer and employee non-solicitation with a solicitation definition drawn to Idaho's affirmative-conduct rule for solicitation, and an optional non-compete gated on the statutory key-employee / key-independent-contractor class, capped at eighteen months absent added consideration, and fitted to the worker's actual geographic and line-of-business footprint. Includes a key-worker recital, a healthcare patient-access clause, mandatory statutory reformation under section 44-2703, and an Idaho-tribunal forum protection under section 29-110. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Idaho-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Idaho practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.description: Legal name of the employer
