fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Keeping ordinary confidential information on a finite, separate track from the perpetual trade-secret obligation avoids a perpetual lid on non-secret information that an Idaho court could treat as a disguised restraint.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. No Idaho statute or appellate decision addresses employee non-solicits, so the clause has no statutory shelter and a tightly-sized duration is its best defense.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and sits comfortably inside the eighteen-month duration presumption if a court analyzes the restraint like a direct-competition covenant.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope, bounding the class to relationships the worker actually held.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under Idaho's Noncompetition Agreement Act, a post-employment non-compete is enforceable only against a key employee or key independent contractor, only to protect the employer's legitimate business interests, and only if reasonable as to duration, geography, and line of business without imposing a greater restraint than reasonably necessary; this template recites the key-worker basis and biases the duration into the eighteen-month presumption window.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. An Idaho non-compete is a statutory creature that binds only a key employee or key independent contractor; include it only after confirming the worker is key, the duration is eighteen months or less or supported by consideration beyond employment, and the geography and work scope match the worker's actual footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits inside the rebuttable presumption that a post-employment term of eighteen months or less is reasonable as to duration. A direct-competition restriction beyond eighteen months requires consideration in addition to employment or continued employment, so the tool should not default past the presumption window.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic areas in which Employee provided services or had a significant presence or influence for Employer
fields.default_value_rationale: Tracks the statutory presumption that a covenant is reasonable as to geographic area when confined to the areas where the key worker provided services or had a significant presence or influence. A company-footprint territory forfeits that presumption.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer, ideally limited to the type of employment or line of business the worker actually conducted for the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors is strong tailoring evidence under Idaho's no-greater-restraint-than-necessary limit.
fields.default_value_rationale: No default competitors are supplied because the list is employer-specific. Counsel should add names only when a named-competitor restriction is a narrower and more accurate fit than the broader Competitive Business definition.

fields.name: idaho_added_consideration
fields.description: Idaho only. If the direct-competition restriction runs longer than eighteen months, Idaho requires consideration in addition to employment or continued employment. State the specific added benefit here (for example, a signing or retention bonus, equity grant, or promotion). If the restriction is eighteen months or less, continued employment alone is sufficient consideration and this field records that the parties rely on it. Shown in the Idaho timing and consideration recital.
fields.default: Employee's employment or continued employment with Employer, which the parties agree is sufficient consideration for a direct-competition restriction of eighteen months or less; if any direct-competition restriction runs longer than eighteen months, the additional consideration described in this agreement
fields.default_value_rationale: Idaho admits no circumstance in which a direct-competition restriction exceeds eighteen months absent consideration beyond employment, while continued at-will employment is sufficient inside the window. Counsel should replace this with the specific negotiated benefit whenever the term exceeds eighteen months.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months keeps the restraint inside the eighteen-month presumption window in case an Idaho court analyzes a no-dealing clause as a direct-competition restriction under chapter 44-27.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives), and it helps keep the covenant from restraining more than reasonably necessary.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months keeps an investment restraint inside the eighteen-month presumption window, since Idaho treats an investment restraint under the same reasonableness limit as any other covenant.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: healthcare_provider
fields.description: Whether Employee is a healthcare provider whose covenant implicates profession-specific restrictions, patient access, or continuity-of-care requirements. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role; do not infer it from the agreement's jurisdiction.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-illinois
items.description: Illinois-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the Illinois Freedom to Work Act (820 ILCS 90): confidentiality and trade-secret protection, customer and employee non-solicitation gated on the $45,000 earnings floor, an optional non-compete gated on the $75,000 earnings floor, the written counsel advisal and 14-day review notice, an adequate-consideration recital, protected-worker exclusions, and discretionary judicial reformation. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Either term is weighed under Illinois' totality-of-the-circumstances reasonableness test, under which duration is one factor balanced against Employer's legitimate business interests.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and a conservative starting point under Illinois' totality-of-the-circumstances reasonableness test.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under the Illinois Freedom to Work Act a non-compete is enforceable only if Employee's actual or expected annualized earnings exceed $75,000 and the covenant is ancillary to a valid employment relationship, supported by adequate consideration, and no greater than required to protect a legitimate business interest judged on the totality of the circumstances; this template recites that basis and keeps the duration within a commonly enforced range.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Non-competes are the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include only after confirming Employee meets the $75,000 earnings threshold, the covenant is supported by adequate consideration, a legitimate business interest exists under Illinois' totality-of-the-circumstances test, and the 14-day advance-notice and attorney-review requirements are satisfied.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is reasonable under Illinois' totality-of-the-circumstances test. The tool should not default to a longer term that a court would find broader than required to protect Employer's legitimate business interests.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Narrower geographic scope is more readily found reasonable under Illinois' totality-of-the-circumstances test; an Illinois court may, in its discretion, reform an overbroad covenant, but extensive judicial reformation is disfavored, so the territory should be drawn to its final intended scope.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: illinois_consideration
fields.description: Illinois only. The professional or financial benefit given in exchange for the restrictive covenants, recited so the consideration is adequate under the Illinois Freedom to Work Act. A benefit relied on as consideration must be expressly tied to the covenant on the face of the document; a benefit described only in a separate offer letter may not support a later integrated covenant. State the specific benefit here (for example, a signing or retention bonus, equity grant, or promotion) unless the agreement instead relies on at least two years of employment after signing. Shown in the Illinois timing and consideration recital when the Illinois projection is used.
fields.default: the compensation, access to Confidential Information, and other benefits Employer provides to Employee under this agreement, which the parties agree are given in exchange for the restrictive covenants
fields.default_value_rationale: The default recites the access-to-confidential-information and compensation that the agreement itself provides and expressly ties them to the covenants, addressing Illinois' express-delineation requirement. Counsel should replace it with the specific negotiated benefit, or rely on the two-year continued-employment benchmark, as appropriate.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-indiana
items.description: Indiana-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Indiana's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Indiana non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint is a disfavored restraint construed strictly against the employer, which must first prove a legitimate protectable interest and then carry the burden of showing the restraint reasonable in time, activity, and geographic area. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection anchored to the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Ind. Code § 24-2-3-2), customer and employee non-solicitation each tied to a protectable interest, and an optional non-compete sized to the employer's actual footprint. Because Indiana's blue pencil is a strict eraser that deletes but never rewrites, and a court cannot add terms even where the agreement purports to authorize it, the covenants are drafted as grammatically severable, independently reasonable units rather than in reliance on judicial reformation. The employee non-solicitation covenant is scoped to a protectable-interest-limited Covered Employees class rather than the workforce at large. A liquidated-damages provision is omitted by default because a sum grossly disproportionate to the loss is an unenforceable penalty. Physician covenants are governed by a layered statute stack keyed to execution date (House Enrolled Act 1004 (2020) content requirements including the reasonable-price buyout; the Senate Enrolled Act 7 (2023) primary care ban; the Senate Enrolled Act 475 (2025) physician-hospital ban). Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Indiana-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Indiana practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: covered_employee_period

In Indiana this look-back is load-bearing: Heraeus voided an employee non-solicitation covenant that reached all of the company's employees, and the eraser rule means a court cannot rewrite an all-employees class into a narrower one.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Indiana has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is a disfavored restraint enforceable only to the extent it protects a legitimate interest and satisfies the Krueger reasonableness test in time, activity, and geography, with both burdens on the employer. This template recites the protectable-interest basis, sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint, and drafts it in severable tiers so an eraser pass leaves a working restraint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most disfavored covenant under the Krueger test, and Indiana's eraser blue pencil means an overreaching version dies rather than shrinks. A customer non-solicit backstopped by the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified protectable interest (confidential information, trade secrets, or customer goodwill), sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint, and — for a physician — confirming the covenant is not statutorily banned.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Indiana imposes no statutory cap on duration; the employer bears the burden of proving the time reasonable under Krueger, so there is no safe-harbor number. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form, stated as its own severable term so an eraser pass can address it in isolation.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Indiana imposes no statutory cap on territory, but the employer must prove the territorial reach reasonable, and an everywhere-the-company-operates footprint rarely survives that showing. A territory drafted in severable tiers lets an eraser pass remove the outer ring without killing the core.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability and gives the eraser blue pencil clearly divisible parts it can strike individually.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A clause that technically forbids holding index funds or ordinary public shares is gratuitous overbreadth an Indiana court will not soften by rewriting.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

fields.name: physician_buyout_price
fields.description: For a physician noncompete entered on or after July 1, 2020, the stated price or price mechanism for the physician's option to purchase a complete and final release from the covenant. The statute requires a reasonable price but does not define one, so a defensible mechanism should be stated rather than left open.
fields.default_value_rationale: Indiana's physician noncompete statute requires the buyout option but does not define a reasonable price, which has become a recurring point of dispute; the price or mechanism is therefore left for counsel to state rather than defaulted to a figure the statute does not supply.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-iowa
items.description: Iowa-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Iowa's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Iowa non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on a three-prong reasonableness test (reasonably necessary to protect the employer, not unreasonably restrictive of the employee, not prejudicial to the public interest), under which the employer seeking enforcement carries the burden of proving reasonableness. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the Iowa Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Iowa Code ch. 550), a continued-employment consideration recital, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete tied to identified protectable interests and sized to the actual footprint, and partial-enforcement reformation that the drafting does not rely on. It also discloses Iowa's narrow occupation and sector rules — the licensed-mental-health-professional practice-restraint ban (Iowa Code § 147.161), the health-care staffing-agency noncompete-and-conversion-fee ban (Iowa Code § 135Q.2), the franchise market-withdrawal nonrenewal disclaimer (Iowa Code § 537A.10), and the University of Iowa Hospitals and Clinics clinical-role prohibition (Iowa Code § 262.9(43)). Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Iowa-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Iowa practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Iowa sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the three-prong Revere Transducers / Lamp reasonableness test rather than a fixed number. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Iowa sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the customer-goodwill interest and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Iowa has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it satisfies the three-prong reasonableness test — reasonably necessary to protect the employer, not unreasonably restrictive of the employee, not prejudicial to the public interest — and is tied to a protectable interest rather than ordinary competition, with the employer seeking enforcement carrying the burden of proving reasonableness. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under the three-prong test, and the employer carries the burden of proving reasonableness; a customer non-solicit backstopped by the Iowa Uniform Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified protectable interest (confidential information, trade secrets, or customer goodwill) and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Iowa imposes no statutory cap on duration; courts weigh time and territory together against the employer's real protectable interest under the three-prong test, so there is no safe-harbor number. Iowa decisions have upheld a one-year restraint on a strong customer-relationship record and rejected a three-year restraint on different facts. 12 months is a common, conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Iowa imposes no statutory cap on territory; a narrower area genuinely needed to protect customer relationships or confidential information is far easier to defend under the three-prong test than a statewide ban. Iowa's reformation cases trim an overbroad territory to the footprint the worker actually served, so an overbroad territory invites the court to cut the covenant down.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-kansas
items.description: Kansas-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Kansas's common-law reasonableness regime: an employee non-compete is enforceable only as an adjunct to a lawful employment contract, when reasonable under the four-factor common-law test (legitimate business interest, no undue burden on the employee, no injury to the public welfare, and reasonable time and territory), with the employer carrying the burden and Kansas appellate courts reviewing enforceability without deference. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection alongside the Kansas Uniform Trade Secrets Act (K.S.A. 60-3320, 60-3326), a continued-at-will-employment consideration recital, a customer non-solicitation covenant drawn to the K.S.A. 50-163(c)(5) two-year material-contact-customer safe harbor, an employee non-solicitation covenant on ordinary reasonableness, an optional non-compete tied to identified protectable interests and sized to the actual footprint, a fixed-term no-open-ended-tolling clause, equitable narrowing that the drafting does not rely on (a court reduces an overbroad restraint but does not rewrite an already-reasonable term, and the K.S.A. 50-163(b) reformation command does not reach non-competes, which the Act excludes at § 50-163(e)(6)), and a physician covenant on the ordinary reasonableness test (no Kansas healthcare non-compete ban). Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Kansas-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Kansas practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Kansas protects non-trade-secret information by contract alone (K.S.A. 60-3326(b)(1)), and a contract term is enforced only as far as it is reasonable, so a finite period is easier to defend than a perpetual lid on ordinary confidential information.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: The Kansas statutory safe harbors run to customer solicitation and owner covenants; they do not create a safe harbor for an employer-employee coworker no-recruit covenant, so this clause stands on the statute's general reasonableness baseline. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, sized to the employer's actual interest in workforce stability.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: An employee customer non-solicitation covenant limited to material contact customers and running no more than two years after employment ends is conclusively presumed enforceable and not a restraint of trade under the Kansas statute. 24 months tracks the outer edge of that statutory safe harbor; counsel should size it down to the actual customer relationships protected, because a term past two years forfeits the presumption and falls back to the general reasonableness baseline.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Kansas enforces an employee non-compete only to the extent it is ancillary to a lawful employment contract and satisfies the four-factor reasonableness test; the Kansas Restraint of Trade Act excludes covenants not to compete, so the statutory presumptions and reformation command do not support it. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under the four-factor reasonableness test; the employer carries the burden and enforceability gets a fresh, unlimited review on appeal. A customer non-solicit drawn to the Kansas statutory safe harbor, backstopped by the Kansas Uniform Trade Secrets Act, often protects the employer with far more certainty. Include only after confirming an identified protectable interest (confidential information, trade secrets, customer relationships, or referral sources) and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Kansas sets no statutory ceiling on a non-compete term; the time factor is weighed against the employer's real protectable interest under the four-factor reasonableness test, so there is no safe-harbor number. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form; Kansas authority has treated a two-year non-compete term as reasonable on its facts but that is fact-specific, not a ceiling.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Kansas imposes no statutory cap on territory; a narrower area genuinely needed to protect the employer's interest is far easier to defend under the four-factor reasonableness test than a statewide ban, and Kansas territorial authority cut an excessive area down to only what was necessary and enforced just that.

fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-kentucky
items.description: Kentucky-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Kentucky's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Kentucky non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the fair-protection standard — fair protection to the employer's interests, no undue hardship on the party restricted, and no interference with the public interests. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection tied to the Kentucky Uniform Trade Secrets Act (KRS 365.880-365.900), a new-consideration recital for mid-employment covenants — continued at-will employment alone is not enough and a bare recital does not cure the gap — customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete tied to identified protectable interests and sized to the actual footprint, discretionary judicial reformation the drafting does not rely on, a Kentucky choice-of-law clause anchored in a genuine connection, a breach-tied extension flagged as an open Kentucky question, and the one categorical statutory ban — KRS 216.724 voids non-compete and buy-out clauses between a health care services agency and its temporary direct care staff. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-louisiana
items.description: Louisiana-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for La. R.S. 23:921's void-unless-excepted rule: confidentiality and trade-secret protection, a customer non-solicitation and an optional non-compete drawn to fit the § 23:921(C) employee exception (named parishes or municipalities, a like business carried on there, and no more than two years from termination), an employee non-solicitation covenant reviewed under the common-law reasonableness standard, an effective-date recital tying the covenants to the start of employment, physician burn-off caps, occupation carve-outs (automobile salesmen, interns and apprentices, real estate licensees), and a Louisiana-law-and-forum selection — with no judicial reformation, because a non-compliant Louisiana covenant is severed rather than rewritten. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Louisiana-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Louisiana practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. In Louisiana the effective date can decide enforceability: the employee exception reaches a person who is employed, and a covenant signed before employment begins has been saved only where the agreement fixes an effective date equal to the worker's first day on the job. Set this on or after the first day of work.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For a Louisiana employee this should be Louisiana: La. R.S. 23:921(A)(2) voids a choice-of-law or choice-of-forum clause in an employment contract unless the employee ratifies it after the dispute arises, so a foreign selection is a red flag rather than a fix.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. In Louisiana a confidentiality lid so broad that honoring it would keep the worker from working in the field risks reading as a restraint on a lawful profession under La. R.S. 23:921(A)(1), so a finite term is preferable to everything-forever.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; either term states the explicit end date Farris requires — the court refused to enforce an anti-poaching clause where the agreement was devoid of any language fixing a duration. Because this covenant sits outside the Noncompetition Agreement Act, the two-year statutory ceiling does not govern it; counsel should still keep the term reasonable, and 12 months is the common lighter alternative.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope, and a class kept to colleagues the departing worker actually worked with supports the Farris reasonableness requirement.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin, and it sits well inside Louisiana's statutory ceiling of two years from termination of employment. Because a non-compliant Louisiana covenant is not reformed but simply fails, the tool defaults inside the statutory window rather than at its outer edge.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the customer non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope, and bounding the customer class to relationships the worker actually served narrows a clause La. R.S. 23:921 already reads strictly.

fields.name: restricted_territory_parishes
fields.description: The specified parish or parishes, municipality or municipalities, or parts thereof — named, not described by radius — where the employer carries on a like business. The Noncompetition Agreement Act requires an enumerated list, and Louisiana case law rejected a 75-mile-radius restriction as overly broad. Applies to the non-compete and the customer non-solicitation.
fields.default_value_rationale: Left for counsel to enumerate. A mileage radius or a catch-all reaching anywhere the employer operates does not satisfy the statute, and because Louisiana courts sever rather than rewrite, an overbroad territory is struck, not trimmed to fit.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under Alabama law an employee may agree to a geographically limited non-compete; this template recites the employee-exception basis, ties the covenant to a listed protectable interest, and biases the duration into the two-year presumptively reasonable window.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Non-competes are the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include only after confirming the restraint fits Alabama's employee exception for a specified geographic area, a competing business, and an existing employment relationship; protects a listed interest; stays within the two-year presumption; will be signed by all parties, including the employer; and does not restrain a member of a recognized profession from practicing that profession. The party seeking enforcement bears the burden of proof on every element.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a common, conservative pick that sits well inside Louisiana's statutory ceiling of two years from termination of employment. A term past two years is not negotiated down by a Louisiana court; a non-compliant covenant may not be reformed and simply fails, so the tool does not default to a term at or beyond the ceiling.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer. La. R.S. 23:921(C) permits restraining only a business similar to that of the employer, so this should describe what the employer concretely does; reaching lines of business the employer never carried on stretches past the exception.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. In a strict-construction regime, binding named competitors instead of an open-ended definition removes ambiguity a court would resolve against enforcement.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

fields.name: worker_is_real_estate_licensee
fields.description: Whether the employee is a real estate broker-licensee. Under La. R.S. 37:1448.1(A) a broker-licensee non-compete is an absolute nullity unless the licensee may rescind it until midnight of the third business day following execution or delivery, whichever is later.
fields.display_label: Employee Is a Real Estate Licensee

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months mirrors the two-year statutory ceiling as a conservative outer bound, though the covenant itself is unaddressed by the statute and its inclusion is a deliberate risk decision.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A clause forbidding ordinary public shares would restrain more than the statutory exception describes.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months mirrors the conservative outer bound used for the other covenants; counsel should confirm the passive-holdings carve-out is intact so the clause does not read as a restraint on a lawful profession.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-maine
items.description: Maine-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for 26 M.R.S. § 599-A: confidentiality and trade-secret protection tied to the statute's three legitimate business interests (trade secrets, non-trade-secret confidential information, and goodwill), an employee non-solicitation covenant, a customer non-solicitation covenant, and an optional non-compete drawn to Maine's reasonableness standard. The agreement recites the § 599-A(4) pre-offer disclosure and three-business-day review window as statutory duties, states the § 599-A(5) delayed-effectiveness mechanics (no effect until the later of one year of employment or six months from signing, except for allopathic and osteopathic physicians), bars the non-compete at or below the 400% federal-poverty-level wage floor, excludes non-owner veterinarians, flags the L.D. 2200 health-care-practitioner restriction for agreements entered into or renewed on or after July 13, 2026, excludes employer-to- employer no-poach terms under § 599-B, keeps Maine law and a Maine forum for Maine-based workers, and drafts each covenant to the restraint the employer actually intends to enforce rather than relying on discretionary judicial reformation. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Maine-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Maine non-compete practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. In Maine the signing date starts the six-month half of the delayed-effectiveness clock, and the date the agreement is entered into or renewed determines whether the L.D. 2200 health-care-practitioner restriction reaches it, so the date carries unusual weight.

fields.name: disclosure_date
fields.description: Date the employer disclosed to the employee or prospective employee that the position will require a non-compete. Maine requires disclosure of the non-compete requirement before the employer makes the offer of employment.

fields.name: delivery_date
fields.description: Date the employer delivered a copy of this agreement to the employee. Maine requires the copy to be provided not less than three business days before the required signing date so the employee has time to review and negotiate.

fields.name: required_signing_date
fields.description: Earliest date on which the employer requires the agreement to be signed. Maine requires this to be at least three business days after the delivery date.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For a Maine-based worker this should remain Maine so the agreement is read against Maine's non-compete disclosure, review-window, and delayed-effectiveness mechanics; a foreign choice-of-law selection for a Maine worker is a localization gap to investigate rather than a fix.

fields.name: wage_floor_reference
fields.description: Reference to the wage floor below which a non-compete is prohibited: 400% of the federal poverty level. The floor is indexed and moves annually; counsel must confirm the controlling dollar figure for the year the covenant is signed (Maine's figure was $60,240 in 2024 and $62,600 in 2025).
fields.default_value_rationale: Maine bans a non-compete for an employee earning wages at or below 400% of the federal poverty level. The dollar equivalent is indexed and updates each year, so the field references the statutory standard rather than a dollar figure that would go stale; counsel confirms the year-of-signing amount.

fields.name: noncompete_effectiveness_reference
fields.description: Statement of when a non-compete takes effect: the later of one year of the employee's employment or six months from the date the agreement was signed, except for an allopathic or osteopathic physician agreement.
fields.default: the later of one year of employment or six months from signing (26 M.R.S. § 599-A(5))
fields.default_value_rationale: Tracks the Maine delayed-effectiveness rule verbatim in effect. The allopathic/osteopathic-physician carve-out from this timing rule is recited in the Non-Competition and physician-notice clauses rather than in the cover term.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Maine enforces confidentiality covenants that protect specialized proprietary work while leaving general skill and knowledge free, so a finite term keeps the non-secret obligation within that line.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Maine names non-solicitation agreements among the lighter alternatives its non-compete framework prefers, so a term sized to the actual workforce interest keeps the clause the lighter tool rather than a de facto hiring ban.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. A Maine customer non-solicit is one of the statute's preferred alternatives but must still serve goodwill or confidential information and sweep no wider than that interest requires, and a shorter term supports that reasonableness showing.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Maine treats a non-compete as contrary to public policy and enforceable only to the extent it is reasonable and no broader than necessary to protect an enumerated legitimate business interest. When included, the covenant is barred at or below the 400% federal-poverty-level wage floor, excluded for non-owner veterinarians, subject to Maine's disclosure and review-window duties and delayed-effectiveness rule, and, for an agreement entered into or renewed on or after July 13, 2026, re-checked under the L.D. 2200 health-care-practitioner restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Maine starts from the position that a non-compete is contrary to public policy and prefers alternative restrictive covenants first. Include only after confirming the worker earns above the 400% federal-poverty-level wage floor, is not a non-owner veterinarian, is not a health-care practitioner reached by L.D. 2200 for an agreement entered into or renewed on or after July 13, 2026, and only after Maine's pre-offer disclosure and three-business-day review window have been satisfied.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. Maine sets no statutory maximum; duration is judged as a fact-intensive reasonableness question considering duration, geographic scope, and the nature of the protected interest, so a modest term supports that showing. The clock also does not begin until the Maine delayed-effectiveness period runs.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which the protected goodwill or confidential information would actually be exploited
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where the protected goodwill or confidential information would actually be exploited rather than the employer's footprint. Maine treats geographic scope as a fact-intensive reasonableness factor and enforces only what sweeps no wider than the protected interest requires.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming the employer's real competitors is the cleanest evidence a Maine covenant is no broader than necessary, which matters because Maine assesses the restraint the employer actually seeks to apply and demands specificity about the protected interest.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives).

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: healthcare_provider
fields.description: Whether Employee is a healthcare provider whose covenant implicates profession-specific restrictions, patient access, or continuity-of-care requirements. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role; do not infer it from the agreement's jurisdiction.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-maryland
items.description: Maryland-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Md. Code, Lab. & Empl. § 3-716: confidentiality and trade-secret protection anchored on the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, an employee non-solicitation covenant, a customer non-solicitation covenant tied to Maryland's protectable client- relationship interest and the statute's client-list carve-out, and an optional non-compete drawn to the common-law reasonableness standard. The agreement recites the statutory worker-class voids that make a covenant null and void outright for low-wage employees (150% or less of the State minimum wage), veterinary practitioners and technicians, and most direct-patient-care Health Occupations Article licensees earning $350,000 or less, and it caps a covered high-earner clinician covenant at one year and ten miles. Consideration rests on continued at-will employment, and each covenant is drafted in severable tiers for Maryland's strict blue-pencil rule rather than relying on discretionary judicial reformation. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Maryland-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Maryland practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. In Maryland the execution date sorts the covenant into a regime: the health-care void and the high-earner clinician cap reach only agreements executed on or after July 1, 2025, and the architect category reaches only agreements executed on or after October 1, 2026, while the veterinary ban reaches backward to existing agreements.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. Maryland records no statutory override of contractual choice of law for restrictive covenants, but the Maryland noncompete statute declares covered covenants null and void as against the public policy of the State, so for a Maryland-based worker a foreign-law selection invites a public-policy fight rather than a clean escape; this should remain Maryland for a Maryland-based worker.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets
fields.default_value_rationale: Maryland's Uniform Trade Secrets Act keys trade-secret status to information that stays not generally known and is subject to reasonable secrecy efforts, with no expiration, so a status-bounded duration captures the full protection the law allows; a shorter fixed term is enforceable but gives up the tail of that protection.

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. A finite term keeps the non-secret obligation sized to the interest behind it, since a perpetual lid on non-secret information reads as overbreadth under the Becker reasonableness test.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Maryland's noncompete statute does not address employee non-solicits, so the clause is judged on ordinary common-law reasonableness, and a relationship-based term supports that characterization.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. Protecting client relationships and client data is squarely inside the interests Maryland recognizes under Becker, and the statute's (a)(2) carve-out spares client-list provisions; a shorter, contact-scoped term keeps the covenant no wider than reasonably necessary.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under the Maryland noncompete statute, a noncompete is null and void outright for whole worker classes: low-wage employees at or under 150% of the State minimum wage, veterinary practitioners and technicians, and most direct-patient-care Health Occupations Article licensees earning $350,000 or less for agreements executed on or after July 1, 2025. It is also capped at one year and ten miles for higher-earning clinicians. A surviving covenant must still satisfy common-law reasonableness. This template recites those gates when the covenant is included.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Run the worker's wage and occupation through the Maryland statutory gates before including a non-compete: the covenant is void regardless of drafting for a low-wage, veterinary, or covered health-care worker, and for a higher-earning clinician it must stay inside the one-year and ten-mile cap. Include only after confirming the worker clears every void class and, where the clinician cap applies, the term and radius stay within it, and after drafting the covenant to the common-law reasonableness standard.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common enforceable duration. Maryland sets no fixed cap for an ordinary employee; the courts apply no arbitrary yardstick. But a covered high-earner clinician covenant may not exceed one year from the last day of employment, so a 12-month default stays within that ceiling.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee actually built customer relationships or used protected information
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where the worker actually built relationships or used protected information rather than the employer's footprint. Maryland calls a restraint overbroad the moment it exceeds what is reasonably necessary to protect the employer's legally protected interest, and for a covered high-earner clinician the territory may not exceed ten miles from the primary place of employment.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming the employer's real competitors is the most concrete evidence a covenant is no broader than reasonably necessary, which matters in Maryland because a court blue-pencils rather than rewrites an overbroad clause into a lawful one.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives), and a clause forbidding ordinary index funds and public shares is gratuitous overbreadth that hands the employee an argument for free.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: healthcare_provider
fields.description: Whether Employee is a healthcare provider whose covenant implicates profession-specific restrictions, patient access, or continuity-of-care requirements. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role; do not infer it from the agreement's jurisdiction.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-massachusetts
items.description: Massachusetts-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement with modular covenant structure (non-compete, non-solicitation, non-dealing, non-investment, confidentiality, non-disparagement). Implements the Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act, Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 149, § 24L (garden-leave or other agreed consideration, 12-month duration cap, formation-timing and notice requirements, excluded-worker categories, choice-of-law and § 24L(f) venue limits, and discretionary judicial reformation), and the profession-specific bans of Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 112, §§ 12X/74D/129B/135C and ch. 149, § 186. Generated by projecting the OpenAgreements restrictive covenant master to the Massachusetts overlay; drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
