fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and a readily enforceable starting point. A customer non-solicitation term is judged for reasonableness rather than capped by statute; adjust it to the employer's actual customer relationships.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. A Massachusetts non-compete is governed by the Noncompetition Agreement Act: enforceable only if no broader than necessary to protect a legitimate business interest, supported by a garden-leave payment or other mutually-agreed consideration, and reasonable in duration, geography, and scope. The operative clauses carry the precise statutory subsection cites.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default: the non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant, is unenforceable against several protected worker categories, and is void for physicians. Include it only after confirming the statutory eligibility, formation, and consideration requirements are met.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: The default is the statutory maximum restricted period; two years is available only on the misconduct exception. The tool must not default above the cap.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Under the Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act a non-compete's geographic reach is presumptively reasonable when limited to areas where the employee provided services or had a material presence in the last two years.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: ma_noncompete_consideration
fields.description: Massachusetts only. The garden leave clause or other mutually-agreed upon consideration that supports the non-compete, stated in the agreement as required by the Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act. A qualifying garden leave clause pays Employee, on a pro-rata basis during the entire restricted period, at least 50 percent of Employee's highest annualized base salary paid within the 2 years before termination. Whether a smaller fixed payment qualifies as "other mutually-agreed upon consideration" is unsettled, so size it conservatively against the garden-leave benchmark and specify the amount here. Shown in Cover Terms and recited in the garden-leave/consideration clause only when noncompete_included is true under the Massachusetts projection.
fields.default: garden leave equal to at least 50% of Employee's highest annualized base salary paid within the two years before termination, paid pro rata across the Restricted Period
fields.default_value_rationale: The 50%-of-base-salary garden leave clause is the only consideration the Noncompetition Agreement Act defines, so it is the conservative default. Counsel should confirm the amount and may substitute other mutually-agreed upon consideration sized against this benchmark; the enforceability of a smaller fixed sum is unsettled.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives).

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-michigan
items.description: Michigan-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for MCL § 445.774a(1): confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MCL §§ 445.1901–445.1910), customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete gated on a reasonable competitive business interest and sized reasonably as to duration, geographic area, and line of business, a consideration recital keyed to Michigan's rule that continued employment supports a covenant only in a genuine at-will setting, a general-knowledge-and-skill carve-out, a paired governing-law and forum-selection provision under which a Michigan court applies Michigan law to the effect of a forum-selection clause, physician proof-of-diversion scrutiny under the physician-patient privilege, and statutory blue-pencil reformation that the drafting does not rely on (MCL § 445.774a(1) empowers a court to limit an unreasonable covenant and enforce it as limited, but the power is discretionary). Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Michigan-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Michigan practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration

fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Michigan enforces confidentiality-based restraints only to the extent they provide reasonable protection for the employer's confidential information, so a finite term for non-secret information is the defensible posture.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Michigan sets no statutory cap on duration; reasonableness turns on the state's general restrictive-covenant reasonableness analysis rather than a fixed number. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative for this least-scrutinized covenant in the family, which counsel should size to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Michigan sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the customer-relationship interest Michigan protects and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Michigan permits an employee non-compete when it protects a reasonable competitive business interest and is reasonable as to its duration, geographical area, and the type of employment or line of business; a covenant that does no more than suppress ordinary competition fails even though Michigan law allows non-competes. This template recites the reasonable-competitive-business- interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under Michigan's restrictive-covenant framework; a customer non-solicit backstopped by the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified reasonable competitive business interest (confidential information, customer relationships, or trade secrets) and sizing the duration, territory, and line of business to the actual footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Michigan imposes no statutory cap on duration; a court weighs duration, geography, and line of business together against the employer's reasonable competitive business interest, so there is no safe-harbor number. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Michigan imposes no statutory cap on territory, but its leading overbreadth decision faulted an unlimited-geography restriction and rejected an unlimited right to restrict clients' business choices; a narrower area genuinely needed to protect the interest is far easier to defend.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors is strong evidence the restraint targets an unfair advantage rather than ordinary competition, and spares the employer from asking a court to exercise its discretionary narrowing power.

fields.name: michigan_consideration
fields.description: Michigan only. The consideration given in exchange for the restrictive covenants, recited so the record shows what moved. Michigan's continued- employment rule treats continued employment as sufficient consideration only in a genuine at-will setting; a covenant signed by a just-cause or contract employee who received only continued employment is void, because refusing to sign would not have been just cause for termination. State the specific benefit here (for example, a signing or retention bonus, equity grant, promotion, or new confidential access) where the worker is not genuinely at will, or rely on continued at-will employment where the worker is. Shown in the Michigan timing and consideration recital.
fields.default: Employee's continued at-will employment, together with the compensation and the access to Confidential Information that Employer provides Employee under this agreement, which the parties agree are given in exchange for the restrictive covenants
fields.default_value_rationale: The default recites continued at-will employment plus the compensation and confidential-information access the agreement itself provides. Continued employment is sufficient only where the worker is genuinely at will; counsel should replace it with a specific negotiated benefit (a raise, bonus, promotion, or new confidential access) whenever the worker has just-cause protection or an employment contract.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-minnesota
items.description: Minnesota-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement centered on confidentiality, trade secret protection, property return, non-disparagement, and narrowly drafted customer and employee non-solicitation. Omits any employee covenant not to compete under Minn. Stat. section 181.988, subd. 2(a), which makes covenants not to compete void and unenforceable, and preserves only the covenants the statute leaves outside its definition of a covenant not to compete under subd. 1(a) — confidentiality and trade-secret protection, non-disparagement, property return, and customer and employee non-solicitation covenants drafted to reach only customers and colleagues the worker actually dealt with, keyed to Employee-initiated solicitation and a defined Restricted Period, so they protect relationships and information rather than functioning as a restraint on competition. Includes Minnesota choice-of-law and forum language reflecting Minn. Stat. section 181.988, subd. 3. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Minnesota-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Minnesota practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For an employee who primarily resides and works in Minnesota, the Minnesota Noncompetition Agreement Act means Minnesota law is the only selection that operates as written for a controversy under that act.
fields.default_value_rationale: Minnesota is selected because Minnesota law supplies the required substantive protection for employees who primarily reside and work in Minnesota in covered restrictive-covenant disputes.

fields.name: governing_venue
fields.description: County and state whose courts are the agreed forum. For a Minnesota-based employee, the Minnesota Noncompetition Agreement Act means a Minnesota forum is the only selection that operates as written for a controversy under that act.
fields.default: the state courts located in Minnesota
fields.default_value_rationale: A Minnesota forum is selected because covered employees who primarily reside and work in Minnesota cannot be required to adjudicate covered controversies outside Minnesota as a condition of employment.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of the non-disparagement covenant

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of the customer non-solicitation restriction, measured from the end of employment. This is a surviving covenant under the Minnesota Noncompetition Agreement Act; keep it a finite, relationship-protective term so the clause does not function as a banned restraint on competition.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin. Minnesota law permits customer-solicitation restrictions only when they remain relationship-protective rather than operating as a substitute restraint on competition, so counsel should shorten or narrow the term if the protected relationships do not justify a full year.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Look-back window before termination used to bound the class of Covered Customers to those the employee actually dealt with or had responsibility for. The Minnesota Noncompetition Agreement Act leaves customer non-solicits lawful, but an all-customers class pushes the clause toward a banned functional non-compete, so the class is tied to a stated look-back window.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a conservative look-back window that limits Covered Customers to recent, relationship-based contacts. Minnesota law does not set this number, so counsel should adjust it to the employee's actual customer responsibilities and the relationships being protected.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of the employee non-solicitation (no-poach) restriction, measured from the end of employment. This is a surviving covenant under the Minnesota Noncompetition Agreement Act; keep it finite and workforce-relationship-protective, not a substitute restraint on the employee's own work.
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Minnesota law does not set a fixed duration, so counsel should size the period to the workforce relationships being protected and avoid using it as a substitute restraint on the employee's own work.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Look-back window before termination used to bound the class of Covered Employees to colleagues the employee actually worked with or supervised. A workforce-wide class adds nothing protective and pushes a lawful no-poach toward the functional-non-compete line the statute polices by effect.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-mississippi
items.description: Mississippi-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Mississippi's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Mississippi non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the three-interest reasonableness balance the courts weigh — the rights of the employer, the rights of the employee, and the rights of the public — with the enforcing employer carrying the burden and the disfavored covenant construed strictly against the drafter. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection independent of any covenant, a continued-at-will-employment consideration recital, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-dealing covenant using the express no-acceptance language a customer-facing restraint needs, an optional non-compete tied to a legitimate protectable interest and sized to the employee's real influence, an express extension-on-breach tolling clause because Mississippi courts do not extend the restricted period on their own but give an express extension clause effect, express and pre-departure assignment to successors, a no-enforcement term for covenants invoked after an arbitrary, capricious, or bad-faith termination, a lawyer practice-restriction exclusion (Miss. R. Prof'l Conduct 5.6), and discretionary equitable reformation that the drafting does not rely on. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Mississippi-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Mississippi practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Mississippi weighs a perpetual lid on ordinary business information against the employee's freedom to work, so a finite term is safer than an indefinite one.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Mississippi sets no statutory cap on duration, and no staged Mississippi authority speaks specifically to employee no-poach covenants, so this restraint stands on ordinary reasonableness under the Texas Road Boring three-interest balance. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin. Mississippi sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the customer-goodwill interest and is often the employer's sturdiest restraint here, because Mississippi recognizes that a properly drafted clause reaching former customers can be reasonable and enforceable (Kennedy). Counsel should size the term to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Mississippi has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is disfavored, construed strictly against the employer, and enforceable only if the employer proves it reasonable in duration, territory, and restrained activity under the three-interest balance (Texas Road Boring) and tied to a legitimate protectable interest rather than ordinary competition. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employee's real influence.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under the Texas Road Boring reasonableness balance; a customer non-solicit backstopped by trade-secret protection often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming a legitimate protectable interest (confidential information, trade secrets, or customer goodwill) and sizing the duration and territory to the employee's real influence rather than the employer's whole footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a common, conservative pick rather than a Mississippi ceiling (Mississippi imposes no statutory cap on duration). Courts weigh time, territory, and activity together under the three-interest balance, so there is no safe-harbor number; counsel should size the default to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form. Note that the restricted period runs from the contractual termination date and a court will not extend it at the back end absent an express extension clause (Frierson).

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Mississippi imposes no statutory cap on territory but measures scope against the employee's real influence: a statewide restraint failed where the employee's protectable relationships were local (Heatherly), while a 250-mile radius held for a telephonic business whose market would have justified even a nationwide limit (Timber Lake). A narrower area genuinely needed to protect customer relationships or confidential information is far easier to defend than a statewide ban.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-missouri
items.description: Missouri-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Missouri's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Missouri non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint begins as a presumptively void restraint on trade and is enforceable only to the extent it is demonstrably reasonable AND protects the employer's trade secrets or customer contacts — never mere competition, with the employer bearing the burden on the protectable interest and on time/geographic reasonableness. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the Missouri Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Mo. Rev. Stat. §§ 417.453, 417.455), an independent-consideration recital reflecting Missouri's divided consideration law, a customer non-solicitation covenant narrowed to serviced customers rather than the whole customer book, an employee non-solicitation covenant drawn to the one-year conclusive-presumption safe harbor of Mo. Rev. Stat. § 431.202, an optional non-compete tied to the identified trade-secret or customer-contact interest and sized to the actual footprint, a tolling clause flagged as an open Missouri question, physician covenants held to the ordinary reasonableness standard (Missouri has no physician-specific statute), and discretionary judicial modification that the drafting does not rely on (Missouri case law permits modification but treats it as discretionary and prices overreach through the prevailing-party fee rule). Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Missouri-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Missouri practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Missouri law gives employee no-hire and anti-raiding covenants a conclusive reasonableness presumption when the postemployment duration is no more than one year. Drawing this covenant to 12 months captures that statutory safe harbor; a longer term falls back to the facts-and-circumstances reasonableness test.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Missouri sets no statutory cap on a customer non-solicit, and the employee no-hire safe harbor does not reach customer restrictions, so the covenant is judged under the common-law reasonableness test. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected; the covered class matters more than the number, because customer restrictions should be limited to customers the employee actually serviced or had substantial contact with.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Missouri has no general non-compete statute; a covenant not to compete begins as a presumptively void restraint on trade and is enforceable only to the extent it is demonstrably reasonable and protects the employer's trade secrets or customer contacts, with the employer bearing the burden on the protectable interest and on time/geographic reasonableness. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under Missouri law, which enforces it only in service of trade secrets or customer contacts and never against mere competition; a customer non-solicit backstopped by the Missouri Uniform Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified trade-secret or customer-contact interest and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Missouri imposes no statutory cap on non-compete duration; restrictions of up to about two years are commonly within the range courts will consider, but that is a framing of case outcomes, not a safe-harbor number, and the employer must still justify the specific term on the facts. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Missouri case law puts the employer to its proof on geographic reasonableness, and a covenant with no territorial limit risks both nonenforcement and no judicial rewrite. A narrower area genuinely needed to protect the trade-secret or customer-contact interest is far easier to defend.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives).

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-montana
items.description: Montana-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Mont. Code Ann. § 28-2-703, where any restraint on a lawful profession, trade, or business is void except a reasonable partial restraint: confidentiality and trade-secret protection, customer and employee non-solicitation drawn as partial restraints, an optional non-compete drawn to Montana's three-part reasonableness test, an independent-consideration recital for post-hire covenants, an employer-initiated-termination acknowledgement, the phased § 28-2-724 health-care provider exclusion, distinct severable promises in place of judicial reformation, and the §§ 28-2-704/705 sale-of-goodwill and partnership-dissolution exception framework. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Montana-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Montana practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: A finite term keeps ordinary confidential information from operating as a perpetual restraint on the trade itself; Montana's framework rewards narrow confidentiality covenants and reserves perpetual protection for trade secrets. The 24-month default is a conservative drafting choice rather than a statutory duration.

fields.name: montana_consideration
fields.description: Montana only. The independent benefit given in exchange for the restrictive covenants when the covenant is signed after employment has begun, recited so the covenant does not rely on continued at-will employment alone. Montana case law treats continued employment alone as potentially insufficient consideration for a post-hire non-compete, and good consideration is also part of Montana's reasonableness test. State the specific benefit here (for example, a raise, a promotion, a signing or retention bonus, an equity grant, or access to trade secrets or Confidential Information tied to the new restriction). A covenant presented during pre-employment negotiations can instead use the job offer itself as the exchange. Shown in the Montana timing and consideration recital.
fields.default: the compensation, access to Confidential Information and trade secrets, and other benefits Employer provides to Employee under this agreement, which the parties agree are given specifically in exchange for the restrictive covenants
fields.default_value_rationale: The default recites the access-to-confidential-information and compensation the agreement itself provides and ties them to the covenants, addressing Montana's independent-consideration requirement for post-hire covenants. Counsel should replace it with the specific negotiated benefit, or rely on the job offer where the covenant is signed as part of pre-employment negotiations.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: No Montana Supreme Court decision fixes a permissible duration for a stand-alone non-solicit, so the covenant rides the Dobbins partial-restraint analysis; a stated, finite period keeps the time limb of that test beyond argument. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should confirm the duration against the worker's actual market.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: A relationship-based class keyed to a recent lookback keeps the restraint partial rather than a workforce-wide hiring ban.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: No Montana decision fixes a permissible non-solicit duration; an enforced Montana customer-restraint pattern used a one-year window keyed to the firm's existing clients. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, and a stated, modest period keeps the reasonableness analysis focused on a limited time period.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: Bounding the class to clients the worker actually served within a recent window keeps the covenant close to the partial-restraint pattern Montana has enforced.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Montana law voids a restraint on a lawful profession, trade, or business except where the covenant is a reasonable partial restraint; a Montana non-compete survives only if it leaves the worker a realistic way to practice and satisfies the state's three-part reasonableness test. This template draws the covenant as a partial restraint and recites that basis.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. In Montana a non-compete that functions as an absolute prohibition on the worker's trade is void outright, and courts do not rewrite an overbroad restraint. Include only after confirming the covenant is genuinely partial, satisfies every element of the state's reasonableness test, and that the worker is not a covered health-care provider under the Montana health-care provider restrictions.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: No Montana statute or decision fixes a permissible non-compete duration; a restraint must be limited in operation as to time or place under the first Dobbins element and must survive the balancing of burden and protection. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, and a modest stated term keeps the time limb defensible. Counsel should size the duration to the worker's actual market.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee actually provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Montana tests territory against the worker's actual market: a territory that blankets everywhere the worker could realistically practice converts a nominally limited clause into an absolute restraint (Curl). Tying scope to the actual service area keeps the covenant partial. Montana courts void rather than trim an overbroad territory.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming real competitors is the cheapest way to prove the restraint is partial — the worker can practice anywhere except a handful of identified firms.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: Non-dealing bars doing the work, not just chasing it, so it edges toward the absolute-prohibition territory the statute voids; a short stated term and a tight customer class keep it defensible as a partial restraint.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives); a clause that forbids ordinary public shares restricts more than any protectable interest requires and pushes a Montana covenant toward the void column.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Rare and deliberate. An investment restriction that reaches how the worker practices or earns in the trade is itself a restraint under Montana law; a stated 12-month term and an intact passive-holdings carve-out keep it framed as partial. The 12-month duration is a conservative drafting choice, not a statutory safe harbor.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: healthcare_provider
fields.description: Whether Employee is a healthcare provider whose covenant implicates profession-specific restrictions, patient access, or continuity-of-care requirements. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role; do not infer it from the agreement's jurisdiction.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-nebraska
items.description: Nebraska-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Nebraska's strict common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Nebraska non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the three-part common-law reasonableness test (not injurious to the public, no greater than reasonably necessary to protect a legitimate employer interest, not unduly harsh and oppressive on the employee). The defining constraint is Nebraska's customer-specific rule: a covenant protecting customer goodwill may be valid only if limited to clients and accounts the worker actually did business with and personally contacted — a rule a 2024 federal decision enforced to reject a non-solicit reaching prospective customers. Nebraska courts do NOT blue-pencil or reform overbroad employment covenants, and nonseverable integrated restraints fall together, so each covenant is drafted self-contained, severable, and scoped to personally served customers from the start rather than in reliance on judicial narrowing. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the Nebraska Trade Secrets Act (Neb. Rev. Stat. §§ 87-501 to 87-507), a customer non-solicitation limited to personally served customers, an optional non-compete tied to that same customer set rather than a market or territory, express merger-by-operation-of-law and assignment terms, and a Nebraska-first choice-of-law posture. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Nebraska-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Nebraska practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional). Title and duties are the first evidence of which customers the worker personally served and what goodwill the worker could unfairly take — the interest a Nebraska customer covenant may protect.

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. The dates anchor the timeline every restraint runs on: when each restricted period starts and ends, and which window of customer contact the covered-customer look-back measures.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For a worker whose services, customers, and competition are centered in Nebraska, plan on Nebraska law controlling whatever law the agreement names. Nebraska courts have applied Nebraska law to an out-of-state employer's covenant on that basis, so a foreign-law selection is a risk variable rather than an enforceability strategy.
fields.default_value_rationale: Nebraska is the conservative default for a Nebraska-centered worker because the governing-law choice should match the state whose law is likely to control the covenant's enforceability.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Nebraska measures every restraint against what is reasonably necessary and refuses to trim the excess, so a finite term keeps an open-ended lid on non-secret information from reading as unjustified breadth.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Nebraska sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the three-part Securities Acceptance reasonableness test rather than a fixed number, and Nebraska courts refuse to reform an overbroad covenant. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope; keeping the class to colleagues the worker actually worked with makes it easier to defend as protecting a legitimate interest.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Nebraska sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit limited to personally served customers maps directly onto the customer-goodwill interest and is the instrument Nebraska courts actually enforce. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common lookback for identifying the personally served customer class. A restriction Nebraska courts upheld was tied to customers the employee personally dealt with during the preceding two years, so counsel may lengthen or shorten this window to match the actual relationships the worker held.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Nebraska has no general non-compete statute; a true non-compete for an ordinary employee is the highest-risk covenant in the suite. A ban framed around a business, market, or territory rather than personally served customers reads as an attempt to prevent ordinary competition, not improper competition. This template ties the covenant to the personally served customer set rather than a geographic or activity ban.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. In Nebraska a customer non-solicit limited to personally served customers, backstopped by the Nebraska Trade Secrets Act, is the enforceable instrument; a whole-market or territory non-compete for an ordinary employee invites the ordinary-competition defect and, because Nebraska courts do not reform overbroad employment covenants, an all-or-nothing loss. Include only after confirming an identified legitimate interest and scoping the restraint to personally served customers.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Nebraska imposes no statutory cap on duration; the three-part reasonableness test weighs the practical effect of the restraint against the employer's legitimate interest, so there is no safe-harbor number. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and personally served customer relationships rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer. Broad definitions that reach beyond the customers the worker actually served are high risk under Nebraska's customer-scope rule, and breadth added here cannot be trimmed by a court later.
fields.default_value_rationale: No default is supplied because the competitive-business definition must be drafted from the employer's actual business and kept subordinate to the personally served customer class.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. When the employer can name its real competitors, binding those names is stronger evidence the restraint is no greater than necessary than an open-ended definition — and narrowing done at the drafting table is the only narrowing this covenant will ever get.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common, conservative duration. Because a non-dealing clause reaches accepting work as well as soliciting it, it presses harder on the harshness prong and should be sized tightly to the personally served customer class it protects.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A clause that technically forbids holding ordinary public shares restrains the worker far beyond any customer relationship or secret, which Nebraska's third requirement — undue harshness on the employee — counts against the covenant.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common, conservative duration. Because a restraint on active competitive roles or material participation functions as a non-compete, it is analyzed under the same three-part reasonableness test and sized no broader than necessary.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-nevada
items.description: Nevada-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for NRS 613.195 and NRS 613.200: confidentiality and trade-secret protection kept inside the NRS 613.200(4) safe harbor, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete drawn to the NRS 613.195(1) four-part validity test with the categorical exclusion of solely hourly-paid workers, the volunteer-customer service carve-out, the post-restructuring continued-pay condition, an acknowledgement of Nevada's mandatory judicial revision regime, and express-consent mechanics for an asset-sale assignment. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Nevada-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Nevada practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: A finite term for non-trade-secret information helps keep the post-termination confidentiality covenant reasonable in scope and duration, as the NRS 613.200(4) safe harbor requires. 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Nevada does not fix a statutory duration for employee non-solicitation, so the covenant is judged on ordinary reasonableness. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative that a court has little reason to revise down.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: No Nevada statute fixes a duration for customer non-solicitation; 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, drawn to the employer's actual customer relationships, and the volunteer-customer carve-out limits its reach regardless of duration.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under NRS 613.195(1) a Nevada non-compete is void and unenforceable unless it is supported by valuable consideration, imposes no restraint greater than required for the employer's protection, imposes no undue hardship on the employee, and imposes restrictions appropriate in relation to the consideration; it may not apply at all to a worker paid solely on an hourly wage basis (NRS 613.195(3)).
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Non-competes are the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include only after confirming the NRS 613.195(1) four-part test is satisfied, the worker is not paid solely on an hourly wage basis (NRS 613.195(3)), and the customer-facing scope preserves the NRS 613.195(2) volunteer-customer carve-out.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Nevada fixes no statutory duration ceiling; the statute authorizes a court to revise an unreasonable time limit down to reasonableness, so the duration should be drawn within the enforceable range at the outset. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable term for a former-employee non-compete.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. A narrower geographic scope is more readily found to impose no greater restraint than required under NRS 613.195(1)(b), and gives a revising court under NRS 613.195(6) little to cut.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: nevada_consideration
fields.description: Nevada only. The valuable consideration given in exchange for the restrictive covenants, recited so the covenant satisfies Nevada's valuable-consideration requirement and so the restrictions can be measured as appropriate in relation to that consideration under Nevada's statutory proportionality test. Continued at-will employment is sufficient consideration at common law, but no Nevada appellate decision squarely decides whether continued employment alone satisfies the statutory proportionality test for a severe restriction, so state the specific benefit here (for example, a signing or retention bonus, equity grant, or promotion) where the restraint is significant. Shown in the Nevada timing and consideration recital.
fields.default: the compensation, access to Confidential Information, and other benefits Employer provides to Employee under this agreement, which the parties agree are given in exchange for, and are appropriate in relation to, the restrictive covenants
fields.default_value_rationale: The default recites the access-to-confidential-information and compensation the agreement itself provides and ties them to the covenants, addressing Nevada's valuable-consideration and proportionality conditions. Counsel should replace it with the specific negotiated benefit where the restraint is significant.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives), and keeps ordinary public-share ownership off Nevada's undue-hardship scale.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.
