fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-new-hampshire
items.description: New Hampshire-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the state's three-part reasonableness test and its statutory traps: confidentiality and trade-secret protection under RSA chapter 350-B, customer and employee non-solicitation tied to the employee's own sphere of customer goodwill, an optional non-compete drawn to the recognized protectable interests, the RSA 275:70 pre-acceptance delivery recital for new hires, the RSA 275:70-a low-wage exclusion, the clinician geographic-practice carve-out (RSA 329:31-a, 326-B:45-a, 326-B:45-b, 315:18), and a good-faith-preserving, drawn-narrow reformation posture rather than reliance on judicial repair. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. New Hampshire-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified New Hampshire non-compete practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. New Hampshire recognizes confidential information short of trade-secret status as protectable (Hobert), but a finite term keeps the obligation within the reasonableness test while trade-secret protection runs with secrecy under RSA 350-B:1.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. New Hampshire measures duration against the interest being protected (Forbes: geography and duration narrowly tailored to the goodwill interest), so counsel should shorten the term where the underlying interest is exhausted sooner.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope. New Hampshire held that ordinary recruiting and hiring cost is not a protectable interest (Olsten), so the class should stay tied to colleagues the departing worker actually worked with.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here; New Hampshire tailors duration to the goodwill interest (Forbes) rather than a round number copied forward.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope. Near limits customer restrictions to the employee's own sphere of customer goodwill, so the lookback should capture only the customers this worker actually served.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under New Hampshire's three-part reasonableness test (Foster) a non-compete is enforceable only if it is no greater than necessary to protect a recognized legitimate interest, imposes no undue hardship on the employee, and is not injurious to the public. It is also subject to two statutory gates: RSA 275:70 (pre-acceptance delivery to a new hire) and RSA 275:70-a (the low-wage ban).
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include it only after confirming a recognized Hobert protectable interest exists, the employee is not a low-wage employee under RSA 275:70-a (hourly rate at or below 200 percent of the federal minimum wage), the RSA 275:70 pre-acceptance disclosure sequence was honored for a new hire, and (for a covered clinician) the RSA 329:31-a / 326-B:45-a / 326-B:45-b / 315:18 geographic-practice ban is addressed.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. New Hampshire requires duration to be narrowly tailored to the goodwill interest (Forbes) and declines to extend a written term where the covenant is unambiguous (Gosselin), so the tool should not default to a longer, harder-to-defend period.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee had customer or patient contact for Employer
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual sphere of customer goodwill. Near struck a covenant reaching customers beyond the salesperson's contacts as more restrictive than necessary, and Forbes requires geography narrowly tailored to the goodwill interest and the worker's actual contact.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability and the good-faith record.

fields.name: new_hampshire_consideration
fields.description: New Hampshire only. The consideration supporting the restrictive covenants, recited so the timing and consideration record is complete. New Hampshire law treats continued employment after signing as consideration for a covenant not to compete, so a mid-employment covenant is supported on consideration grounds. Consideration does not, however, cure a timing defect: for a new hire required to sign as a condition of employment, the New Hampshire pre-acceptance disclosure rule makes an undisclosed noncompete unenforceable regardless of consideration. State the specific benefit here (for example, initial or continued employment, a signing or retention bonus, an equity grant, or a promotion). Shown in the New Hampshire timing and consideration recital.
fields.default: Employee's employment or continued employment with Employer, together with the compensation and access to Confidential Information that Employer provides under this agreement, which the parties agree are given in exchange for the restrictive covenants
fields.default_value_rationale: The default recites employment or continued employment, a consideration basis New Hampshire accepts for a covenant not to compete, together with the compensation and confidential-information access the agreement itself provides. Counsel should replace it with the specific negotiated benefit where one exists.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common enforceable duration. A non-dealing restraint drifts further from the worker's own customer influence than a non-solicit, so New Hampshire's no-greater-than-necessary prong (Foster) counsels a conservative term.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A carve-out that technically forbids index funds and ordinary public shares adds gratuitous weight to the undue-hardship prong of the Foster test.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common enforceable duration. An investment restraint detached from customer-facing activity sits poorly with a test that asks which recognized employer interest the restriction protects (Foster, Hobert).

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-new-jersey
items.description: New Jersey-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for New Jersey's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general New Jersey non-compete statute for the ordinary workforce, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the three-part common-law reasonableness test (protects the employer's legitimate interests, imposes no undue hardship on the employee, and is not injurious to the public). It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the New Jersey Trade Secrets Act (N.J.S.A. 56:15-1 et seq.), a continued-employment consideration recital, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete tied to identified legitimate interests and sized to the actual footprint, a breach-tied tolling clause, physician public-interest scrutiny, and partial enforcement that works with the grain of the doctrine. It carves out the discrimination-claim concealment bar (N.J.S.A. 10:5-12.8) and disclaims practice restrictions for lawyers (RPC 5.6) and client-access restrictions for psychologists (N.J.A.C. 13:42-10.16). Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. New Jersey-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified New Jersey practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: New Jersey sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the holistic Solari/Whitmyer reasonableness test rather than a fixed number. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual legitimate interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: New Jersey sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the employer's customer-relationship interest and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. New Jersey has no general non-compete statute for the ordinary workforce; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it satisfies the three-part common-law reasonableness test and is tied to a legitimate interest rather than ordinary competition. This template recites the legitimate-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most closely scrutinized covenant under the common-law reasonableness test; a customer non-solicit backstopped by the New Jersey Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified legitimate interest (confidential business information, trade secrets, or customer relationships) and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: New Jersey imposes no statutory cap on duration; courts weigh time and territory together against the employer's real legitimate interest under the common-law reasonableness test, so there is no safe-harbor number, though durations in the one-to-two-year range are commonly upheld where the geography is tailored. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. New Jersey imposes no statutory cap on territory; courts weigh territory against the public's access to the service and will shrink a radius that overreaches, so a narrower area genuinely needed to protect customer relationships or confidential information is far easier to defend under the common-law reasonableness test than a statewide ban.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-new-mexico
items.description: New Mexico-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the common-law reasonableness standard and the health-care practitioner statute (NMSA 1978, §§ 24A-4-1 to 24A-4-5): confidentiality and trade-secret protection tied to a specific legitimate interest, an employee non-solicitation covenant, a customer non-solicitation covenant, and an optional non-compete drawn to reasonableness in time, geography, and scope rather than copied from a form. The agreement documents independent new consideration for a covenant signed after employment begins, because New Mexico treats continued at-will employment as illusory. It excludes non-competes for the ten covered health-care practitioner classes, holds the surviving practitioner fallbacks — one-year patient and employee non-solicits, early-departure repayment, NDAs, and reasonable liquidated damages — within their statutory bounds, keeps New Mexico law and a New Mexico forum for agreements covering clinical health-care services rendered in the state, and secures narrowing through an express reformation clause because New Mexico's only staged reformation authority is contract-based. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. New Mexico-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified New Mexico practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name

fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. The execution date does statutory work in New Mexico: for psychologists, physician assistants, and pharmacists, the health-care practitioner article applies only to agreements, renewals, or extensions executed on or after the effective date of the 2023 act.

fields.name: covenant_timing
fields.description: Whether this agreement is signed as a condition of the initial offer of employment or after employment has already begun. New Mexico treats continued at-will employment as an illusory promise that cannot be consideration, so a mid-stream covenant must be supported by independent new consideration.
fields.default: at the outset of employment as a condition of the offer
fields.default_value_rationale: For a covenant signed as a condition of initial hire, the offer of employment itself can serve as consideration. Replace this with a description tied to the new consideration (raise, bonus, promotion, or equity grant) when the covenant is signed after employment began.

fields.name: independent_consideration
fields.description: The bargained-for benefit that supports this agreement. For a mid-stream covenant, describe the new consideration (a raise, bonus, promotion, equity grant, or other benefit) dated to signing, because a bare recital of continued at-will employment is illusory under New Mexico law.
fields.default: the offer of employment and Employee's access to Employer's Protected Interests
fields.default_value_rationale: For an at-hire covenant the offer of employment supplies consideration. For a covenant signed after employment began, replace this with the specific new benefit exchanged for the covenant.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For an agreement covering clinical health-care services to be rendered in New Mexico, a provision choosing another state's law is void, unenforceable, and against public policy, so this should remain New Mexico for such an agreement.

fields.name: venue
fields.description: Forum for disputes. For an agreement covering clinical health-care services rendered in New Mexico, a provision requiring litigation in another state is void, so the forum should remain New Mexico for such an agreement.
fields.default: the state and federal courts located in New Mexico

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. A finite term keeps the non-secret obligation from operating as an indirect restraint on the general skills and knowledge New Mexico refuses to protect.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common enforceable duration and stays within the one-year ceiling the health-care practitioner statute preserves for employee non-solicits, so the same term is safe whether or not the worker is a covered practitioner.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common enforceable duration and stays within the one-year ceiling the health-care practitioner statute preserves for patient non-solicits.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. New Mexico enforces a non-compete when a court deems it reasonable in time, geography, and scope and tied to a legitimate business interest. It is unenforceable, however, where it restricts a covered health-care practitioner's clinical services in New Mexico. This template recites the reasonableness basis and the practitioner exclusion when the covenant is included.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. A New Mexico non-compete must be justified on the facts of the particular case, and no New Mexico court is obliged to narrow an overbroad covenant absent express contract language authorizing it. Include only after confirming the worker is not one of the ten covered health-care practitioner classes and the time, territory, and scope are matched to an identified protectable interest.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. New Mexico tests duration on the facts of the particular case with no fixed cap and no safe harbor so the period should be connected to the interest it protects rather than copied from a form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee actually worked or had material responsibility for Employer during employment
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where Employee actually worked rather than the employer's footprint. A New Mexico district court cut a one-hundred-mile dental restriction to thirty miles; geography sized to the worker's real territory is the least vulnerable to that treatment.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming the employer's real competitors is strong evidence the covenant protects an identified interest rather than punishing departure, which is exactly the showing the New Mexico reasonableness test rewards.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-new-york
items.description: New York-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for New York's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general New York non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the three-pronged common-law reasonableness test, and a single failed prong invalidates the covenant. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection, a continued-employment consideration recital, customer and employee non-solicitation with the customer non-solicit narrowed to customers the employee actually served, an optional non-compete tied to New York's short list of recognized protectable interests, a broadcast-employee exclusion under N.Y. Labor Law § 202-k, a sale-of-business covenant aligned with New York's implied goodwill covenant, a governing-law clause drafted against New York's public-policy limit on foreign choice-of-law, and a drafting posture that sizes each covenant to the protectable interest rather than relying on discretionary partial enforcement. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.

fields.default_value_rationale: New York sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the holistic BDO Seidman reasonableness test rather than a fixed number, and New York treats employee non-recruitment clauses as inherently more reasonable and less restrictive than non-competes. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, sized to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin. New York sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the client-goodwill interest BDO Seidman recognizes and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete, so counsel should size the term to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope. Under Brown & Brown, the class must reach only customers the employee actually developed or serviced, so the lookback pairs with a material-contact limit rather than sweeping in the entire book of business.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. New York has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it satisfies every prong of the common-law reasonableness test and is tied to a recognized protectable interest rather than ordinary competition. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under New York's reasonableness test, and any single failed prong invalidates it; a customer non-solicit tied to serviced relationships often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming a recognized New York interest (trade secrets, confidential customer information, unique or extraordinary services, or client goodwill) and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint. Do not impose a post-employment non-compete on a broadcast employee.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a common, conservative pick rather than a New York ceiling (New York imposes no statutory cap on duration). Courts weigh time and territory together against the employer's real protectable interest under BDO Seidman, so there is no safe-harbor number; counsel should size the default to the actual role and market. The pending S4641A bill would cap any permissible non-compete at one year if it ever becomes law (pending only; not law).

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. New York imposes no statutory cap on territory, but geographic scope is one of the express reasonableness factors; an area genuinely needed to protect customer relationships or confidential information is far easier to defend under BDO Seidman than an everywhere-we-operate territory, which invites a finding of overbreadth a New York court has discretion to leave unrepaired.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors is strong evidence the covenant reaches no further than the protectable interest requires, and it supports the good-faith, no-overreaching record on which any discretionary partial enforcement depends.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A carve-out below a stated threshold keeps a passive index-fund holding from adding hardship on the undue-hardship prong without protecting any recognized interest.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-north-carolina
items.description: North Carolina-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for North Carolina's common-law reasonableness regime with a strict-severance twist: there is no general North Carolina non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the five-element common-law test — in writing, part of an employment contract, based on valuable consideration, reasonable as to time and territory, and designed to protect a legitimate business interest and not against public policy — read strictly against the drafter against the backdrop of N.C. Gen. Stat. § 75-1's declaration that contracts in restraint of trade are illegal. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act (N.C. Gen. Stat. §§ 66-152, 66-154), a new-consideration recital for mid-employment covenants, a § 75-4 signed-writing acknowledgement keyed to the employee's signature, customer and employee non-solicitation confined to actual relationships, an optional non-compete confined to the employee's actual duties and customer territory, an express extension-on-breach tolling clause, physician public-health scrutiny, an in-state forum required by N.C. Gen. Stat. § 22B-3, and severable, tiered covenants that do not rely on judicial reformation because North Carolina follows the strict blue-pencil doctrine under which a court may strike but never rewrite. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. North Carolina-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified North Carolina practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: mid_employment_consideration
fields.description: The new consideration supporting the covenants where the employee signs after employment has already begun — for example a raise in pay, a new job assignment, or a stated one-time payment. Leave blank where the agreement is signed at the outset of employment, in which case the offer of employment is itself the consideration.
fields.default_value_rationale: North Carolina's mid-employment consideration rule requires new consideration for a covenant signed after the employment relationship already exists; continued at-will employment is not enough. The amount need not be large — courts do not weigh adequacy and $500 has sufficed — but it must actually move and should be recited. Left blank by default because a covenant signed at hire needs no separate payment; the offer of new employment is itself valuable consideration.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. North Carolina construes restraints strictly against the drafter and never trims them, so a perpetual lid on ordinary business information reads as overreach.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: North Carolina sets no statutory cap on duration; an employee non-solicit has no statute of its own and stands or falls on ordinary restraint-of-trade reasonableness. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: North Carolina sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the customer-goodwill interest and is often the sturdiest restraint when held to actual relationships. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. North Carolina has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only if it satisfies all five elements of the common-law reasonableness test and is tied to a legitimate business interest rather than ordinary competition. This template confines the restraint to the employee's actual duties and customer territory and drafts it as a severable unit because North Carolina courts strike but never rewrite an overbroad covenant.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant, one defective element defeats it, and a court will not resize an overbroad restraint — it strikes it. A customer non-solicit backstopped by the North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming a legitimate interest (intimate knowledge of the business not generally available to the public) and confining duration, territory, and activity to the employee's actual role and customer footprint.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: North Carolina imposes no statutory cap on duration; reasonableness is a question of law weighing time and territory together on a sliding scale, and a court will not shorten an unreasonable term — it declines the covenant. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services and built customer relationships
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where the employee actually worked and built customer relationships. A territory is reasonable only to the extent it protects the employer's interest in maintaining its customers, so a footprint-wide or aspirational territory is among the most reliable ways to lose the whole covenant to a strike.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer, defined by reference to the duties the employee actually performed rather than every line of the employer's business.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Binding the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). Because a North Carolina court strikes rather than narrows an overbroad restraint, a clean passive-holdings carve-out avoids gratuitous overbreadth like a technical ban on index funds and ordinary public shares.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-north-dakota
items.description: North Dakota-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement centered on confidentiality, trade secret protection, property return, non-disparagement, and a narrowly drafted anti-raiding employee non-solicitation covenant. Omits any employee covenant not to compete, any customer non-solicitation covenant, and any non-dealing covenant, because N.D. Cent. Code § 9-08-06 makes a contract that restrains a lawful profession, trade, or business to that extent void, and North Dakota case law treats post-employment customer solicitation and replacement restrictions as void with no statutory exception. It preserves only the covenants North Dakota law leaves enforceable — confidentiality and trade-secret protection under N.D. Cent. Code ch. 47-25.1, non-disparagement, property return, and a narrow anti-raiding employee non-solicitation covenant North Dakota case law treats as not void as a restraint of trade — each drawn to reach only Covered Employees the worker actually dealt with, keyed to Employee-initiated solicitation and a defined Restricted Period, so it protects the workforce and information rather than functioning as a restraint on competition. Includes North Dakota choice-of-law and forum language reflecting North Dakota public policy against enforcing a non-compete through a foreign choice-of-law or forum selection and N.D. Cent. Code § 28-04.1-03. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. It anchors the surviving obligations — the confidentiality term and the anti-raiding non-solicitation window — without changing the North Dakota rule that voids restraints on a lawful profession, trade, or business regardless of the date signed.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For an employee who primarily resides and works in North Dakota, North Dakota law is the selection that operates as written for a controversy over an alleged restraint of trade.
fields.default_value_rationale: North Dakota is selected because, for a North Dakota worker, choosing another state's law to enforce a restraint can fail against North Dakota public policy.

fields.name: governing_venue
fields.description: County and state whose courts are the agreed forum. For a North Dakota-based employee, a North Dakota forum is the selection most likely to operate as written; a North Dakota court may decline an exclusive foreign forum that is unfair or unreasonable.
fields.default: the state courts located in North Dakota
fields.default_value_rationale: A North Dakota forum keeps disputes involving a North Dakota worker in the forum whose public policy controls whether an alleged restraint operates.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of the non-disparagement covenant

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of the employee non-solicitation (anti-raiding) restriction, measured from the end of employment. Keep it finite and workforce-relationship-protective, not a substitute restraint on the employee's own work; North Dakota treats only a narrow anti-raiding formulation as outside the void restraint rule.
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. North Dakota's law-backed limit is the narrow anti-raiding shape of the covenant, not a specific duration, so either finite term rides on keeping that shape.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Look-back window before termination used to bound the class of Covered Employees to colleagues the employee actually worked with or supervised. A workforce-wide class adds nothing protective and pushes a lawful anti-raiding clause toward the kind of restraint North Dakota voids.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a conservative look-back period that ties the covered class to recent working relationships instead of sweeping in the employer's entire workforce.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-ohio
items.description: Ohio-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Ohio's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Ohio non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the three-part reasonableness test (no greater than required to protect the employer, no undue hardship on the employee, not injurious to the public). It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection under the Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act (R.C. 1333.61-1333.69), a continued-at-will-employment consideration recital, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete tied to identified protectable interests and sized to the actual footprint, a litigation-extension tolling clause, express assignment beyond a statutory merger, heightened public-interest scrutiny for physician covenants, and discretionary judicial reformation that the drafting does not rely on. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-oklahoma
items.description: Oklahoma-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement centered on confidentiality, trade secret protection, property return, non-disparagement, and narrowly drafted customer and employee non-solicitation. Omits any employee covenant not to compete, because Okla. Stat. tit. 15, § 217 voids every contract restraining a person from exercising a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind, save for the narrow statutory carve-outs in §§ 218-219 (sale of goodwill and partnership dissolution) and §§ 219A-219B. The agreement preserves only the covenants those sections leave standing for an employee: a customer non-solicitation covenant drafted to reach only the direct solicitation of the sale of goods or services from the former employer's established customers, as § 219A(A) permits and no further; an employee anti-raiding covenant that § 219B removes from the restraint-of-trade prohibition; and confidentiality, trade-secret, property-return, and non-disparagement obligations that restrain the misuse of information rather than the exercise of a trade. Both non-solicits are keyed to a defined Restricted Period and expressly disclaim any bar on Employee working for a competitor. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Oklahoma-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Oklahoma practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. Oklahoma's void rule for employee non-competes does not turn on a date floor, but a stated effective date fixes when the surviving covenants begin and anchors any consideration analysis for a covenant signed after employment began.
fields.default_value_rationale: No default is supplied because the effective date is transaction-specific. The field should be completed from the parties' signing or intended start date rather than inferred from Oklahoma law.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For an employee who resides and works in Oklahoma, an Oklahoma forum court will not apply chosen foreign law where doing so violates Oklahoma's public policy against restraints on work, so an Oklahoma selection is the one most likely to operate as written for an Oklahoma employee-restrictive-covenant dispute heard in Oklahoma.
fields.default_value_rationale: Oklahoma is the correct default for an Oklahoma employee because the governing-law choice should align with the forum most likely to apply Oklahoma's public-policy limits rather than invite an out-of-state law fight.

fields.name: governing_venue
fields.description: County and state whose courts are the agreed forum. A mandatory out-of-state forum clause is the operative enforcement risk in Oklahoma: a court applying another state's law may enforce a covenant Oklahoma would void, so an Oklahoma forum keeps the dispute before a court expected to apply Oklahoma's void rule.
fields.default: the state courts located in Oklahoma
fields.default_value_rationale: Oklahoma state courts are the default venue because the key enforcement risk is transfer to an out-of-state forum that may apply another state's law to covenants Oklahoma would treat as void.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of the non-disparagement covenant

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of the customer non-solicitation restriction, measured from the end of employment. This is a surviving covenant that Oklahoma permits only to the extent it bars direct solicitation of established customers. Oklahoma fixes no statutory maximum, so keep it reasonable under the common-law rule of reason; practitioners commonly use two years or less.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin. Oklahoma supplies no statutory maximum, but the term should remain reasonable and comfortably within the commonly used two-years-or-less drafting range.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Look-back window before termination used to bound the class of Covered Customers to the established customers of the employer the employee actually dealt with. Section 219A(A) ties the permitted restriction to the established customers of the former employer, so the class is tied to a stated look-back window rather than reaching prospective or former customers.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of the employee anti-raiding (no-poach) restriction, measured from the end of employment. Oklahoma removes an employee-solicitation covenant from the restraint-of-trade prohibition; keep it finite and workforce-relationship-protective and measure its length against the rule of reason.
fields.default_value_rationale: Oklahoma permits the solicitation-focused covenant shape but does not set a duration. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should shorten or justify the period based on the workforce relationship being protected.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Look-back window before termination used to bound the class of Covered Employees to colleagues the employee actually worked with or supervised. A workforce-wide class adds nothing protective; Oklahoma protects against soliciting the business's employees and independent contractors.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a conservative look-back window that keeps the covered class tied to employees and independent contractors with a recent workplace connection to Employee rather than the entire workforce.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-oregon
items.description: Oregon-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for ORS 653.295's void-unless regime: confidentiality and trade-secret protection, customer and employee non-solicitation and non-dealing covenants that fall outside the statutory checklist, and an optional non-compete gated on the two-week written-offer notice or bona fide advancement, exempt-salaried status above the inflation-indexed salary threshold, a statutory protectable interest, the 12-month cap, and delivery of a signed copy within 30 days after termination, with a garden-leave pay-to-enforce alternative for sub-threshold workers, the 2025 medical-licensee ban, the Workplace Fairness Act carve-out, and a draft-within-the-statute posture rather than reliance on judicial reformation. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Oregon-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Oregon practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements, and it tracks Oregon's protectable-interest line between trade secrets (perpetual) and competitively sensitive confidential information that would not qualify as a trade secret under ORS 653.295(2).

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Oregon carves employee non-solicits out of the statutory non-compete checklist entirely, so the duration is judged under common-law reasonableness rather than the 12-month statutory cap that governs the non-compete.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements. Oregon carves customer non-solicits out of the statutory non-compete checklist, so the clause is judged under common-law reasonableness.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope, and it keeps the covered class tied to active, ongoing customer relationships.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under ORS 653.295 an Oregon employee non-compete is void and unenforceable unless every statutory condition is met — a two-week written-offer notice or a bona fide advancement, exempt-salaried status above the inflation-indexed threshold (or the garden-leave alternative), a statutory protectable interest, a term no longer than 12 months from termination, and delivery of a signed copy within 30 days after termination.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. In Oregon a non-compete starts from a void-unless default; include it only after confirming the formation-timing gateway, the exempt-status and salary threshold (or a written garden-leave commitment), a statutory protectable interest, and that the worker is not a medical licensee barred under ORS 653.297.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Oregon caps the non-compete term at 12 months from the date of termination and voids the excess; 12 months is therefore the statutory ceiling, not merely a conservative choice. Any longer term is dead text under Oregon law.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. ORS 653.295 does not prescribe a territory limit, but the protectable-interest condition does the same work: a footprint that outruns the trade secrets and strategic plans the worker touched reads as a restraint on employment, and there is no judicial rewrite waiting to save an overbroad covenant in Oregon.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: oregon_garden_leave_pay
fields.description: Oregon only. The employer's written garden-leave payment commitment used to enforce a non-compete against an employee who does not satisfy the exempt-status or salary-threshold conditions of ORS 653.295(1)(b) and (e). ORS 653.295(7) enforces the covenant for the full term of the agreement, for up to 12 months, only if the employer agrees in writing to pay, for the time the employee is restricted from working, the greater of (a) at least 50 percent of the employee's annual gross base salary and commissions at the time of termination or (b) 50 percent of the inflation-indexed threshold figure. State the specific payment commitment here. Shown in the Oregon garden-leave cover-term row and recited in the garden-leave clause when the non-compete is included.
fields.default: payment to Employee, for the time Employee is restricted from working, of the greater of (a) at least 50 percent of Employee's annual gross base salary and commissions at the time of termination or (b) 50 percent of the inflation-indexed threshold amount published by the Oregon Bureau of Labor and Industries for the calendar year preceding termination
fields.default_value_rationale: The default recites the two halves of the ORS 653.295(7) formula so the pay-to-enforce path is available where the employee falls below the salary threshold. Counsel should confirm the payment is actually committed in writing; the garden-leave path overrides only subsections (1)(b) and (e) — the notice-or-advancement gateway, protectable interest, and 30-day delivery conditions still apply.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common enforceable duration. ORS 653.295(5)(b) exempts covenants not to solicit or transact business with customers of the employer from the non-compete checklist, so the non-dealing form is judged under common-law reasonableness.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives).

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the ORS 653.295(3) non-compete cap, because an investment restraint that operates like a restraint on working enjoys none of the ORS 653.295(5) carve-out protection and is tested against the statutory gates.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: healthcare_provider
fields.description: Whether Employee is a healthcare provider whose covenant implicates profession-specific restrictions, patient access, or continuity-of-care requirements. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role; do not infer it from the agreement's jurisdiction.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-pennsylvania
items.description: Pennsylvania-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Pennsylvania's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Pennsylvania non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on Pennsylvania's four-part common-law test — ancillary to an employment relationship, supported by adequate consideration, reasonably limited in duration and geography, and tied to a legitimate protectable interest. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection, a new-consideration recital for a covenant signed after employment begins that does not lean on a Uniform Written Obligations Act recital, customer and employee non-solicitation drafted as affirmative-outreach restraints with a geographic limit, an optional non-compete tied to identified protectable interests and sized to the actual footprint under close scrutiny, a health care practitioner clause reflecting the Fair Contracting for Health Care Practitioners Act (Act 74 of 2024) one-year cap, dismissal rule, and 90-day patient-notice duty, an express assignability provision the covenant needs to reach a buyer, and separable, tiered restraints that do not rely on discretionary judicial reformation. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Pennsylvania-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Pennsylvania practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.description: The new and valuable consideration supporting the covenants where this agreement is signed after employment has already begun (for example, a signing bonus, raise, promotion, or other beneficial change in employment status). Continued at-will employment alone is not adequate consideration for a mid-employment covenant in Pennsylvania.
fields.display_label: New Consideration for a Mid-Employment Covenant
fields.default_value_rationale: Empty by default because a covenant signed at the outset of employment is supported by the offer and commencement of employment and needs no separate new consideration. A covenant an existing at-will employee signs after employment began requires new and valuable consideration beyond continued employment; if this agreement is signed mid-employment, counsel must record the specific benefit actually delivered here.

fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Pennsylvania sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the four-part Socko reasonableness test rather than a fixed number. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin. Pennsylvania sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the customer-goodwill interest and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete, so counsel should size the term to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Pennsylvania has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it satisfies Pennsylvania's four-part reasonableness test and is tied to a legitimate protectable interest rather than ordinary competition. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint. A non-compete against a covered health care practitioner is separately limited by the Fair Contracting for Health Care Practitioners Act.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant under Pennsylvania's four-part test; a customer non-solicit backstopped by trade-secret protection often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified protectable interest (confidential information, trade secrets, or customer goodwill), sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint, and confirming the worker is not a health care practitioner covered by the Fair Contracting for Health Care Practitioners Act.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a common, conservative pick rather than a Pennsylvania ceiling (Pennsylvania imposes no statutory cap for the ordinary workforce). Courts weigh time and territory together against the employer's real protectable interest under Socko and Sidco, so there is no safe-harbor number; counsel should size the default to the actual role and market. For a covered health care practitioner, Act 74 caps any enforceable non-compete at one year and voids it entirely if the employer dismissed the practitioner.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Pennsylvania imposes no statutory cap on territory; a narrower area genuinely needed to protect customer relationships or confidential information is far easier to defend under the Socko and Sidco reasonableness analysis than a statewide ban, and an overbroad territory invites a court to enforce less than the employer drafted, or nothing at all.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
