items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-rhode-island
items.description: Rhode Island-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the Rhode Island Noncompetition Agreement Act (R.I. Gen. Laws ch. 28-59) and the common-law reasonableness regime: confidentiality and trade-secret protection preserved by the act, an employee non-solicitation covenant, a customer non-solicitation covenant, and an optional non-compete drafted to the state's strict-scrutiny reasonableness standard. The act bans non-competes outright for four protected worker categories — employees who are FLSA-nonexempt, undergraduate or graduate student interns, workers age 18 or younger, and low-wage employees earning not more than 250 percent of the federal poverty level for individuals — and separate statutes void any restriction of a physician's or an advanced practice registered nurse's right to practice. Employee and customer non-solicits and confidentiality agreements are excluded from the statutory non-compete definition, so each restraint is drafted as the covenant type it actually is and runs through common law. The agreement recites the common-law enforcement elements, permits partial enforcement while drawing each covenant no broader than reasonably necessary, and relies on trade-secret protection as the fallback the act preserves. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Rhode Island-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Rhode Island practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement. In Rhode Island the execution date fixes which current statutes and figures frame the covenant: the APRN practice-restriction ban took effect June 17, 2024, and the low-wage category is indexed to 250 percent of the federal poverty level for individuals, which changes over time.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. The Rhode Island practice note records no Rhode Island anti-evasion statute and no controlling choice-of-law decision for restrictive covenants, so a foreign-law clause covering a Rhode Island worker is unsettled risk in both directions; this should remain Rhode Island for a Rhode Island-based worker.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. The Noncompetition Agreement Act excludes nondisclosure and confidentiality agreements from the non-compete definition only so long as the clause stays a confidentiality clause; a finite term for ordinary information keeps the NDA from reading as a hidden activity restraint.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Rhode Island excludes employee non-solicits from the statutory non-compete definition, routing the clause to the common-law reasonableness test, where a tightly-sized term supports the no-broader-than-apparently-necessary showing.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here and stays comfortably inside the 24-month customer restraint the Rhode Island Supreme Court most recently enforced at the preliminary-injunction stage; duration is still measured against the legitimate interest it protects, so a shorter term eases the reasonableness showing.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Rhode Island permits non-competes for workers outside the protected categories, but the Noncompetition Agreement Act makes the covenant unenforceable against an FLSA-nonexempt employee, a student intern, a worker age 18 or younger, or a low-wage employee, and profession-specific bans void it for physicians and APRNs. For every worker who clears those gates it remains a disfavored restraint subject to strict judicial scrutiny.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. A Rhode Island non-compete is unenforceable outright against four protected worker categories and void for physicians and APRNs, and even for other workers it is disfavored and strictly scrutinized. Include only after confirming the worker is outside every protected category, is not a physician or APRN, and the covenant is ancillary to a valid relationship, supported by consideration, protective of an identified legitimate interest, and drawn no broader than apparently necessary under Rhode Island common law.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. Rhode Island measures duration against the legitimate interest the restraint protects under strict scrutiny, so a shorter, interest-justified period is easier to defend than a longer one.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employer's protected legitimate interests actually operate
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where Employer's protected interests actually operate rather than an open-ended footprint. Rhode Island tolerates even a restraint with no stated limits, but only to the extent necessary to protect the promisee's legitimate interests, so an everywhere territory invites the court to cut the covenant down to what the employer can prove.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming the employer's real competitors is strong evidence the covenant extends no further than apparently necessary, the exact measure Rhode Island common law applies to these provisions.
fields.default_value_rationale: Blank is the conservative default because a named-competitor list should be supplied only when the employer can identify actual competitors whose inclusion helps narrow the restraint.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A carve-out below a stated threshold keeps the covenant from restraining ordinary public-share ownership no legitimate interest requires — gratuitous overbreadth in a state that enforces restraints only as far as apparently necessary.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: healthcare_provider
fields.description: Whether Employee is a healthcare provider whose covenant implicates profession-specific restrictions, patient access, or continuity-of-care requirements. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role; do not infer it from the agreement's jurisdiction.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-south-carolina
items.description: South Carolina-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for South Carolina's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general South Carolina non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on a conjunctive five-factor reasonableness test — necessary to protect a legitimate interest, reasonably limited in time and place, not unduly harsh and oppressive, reasonable from the standpoint of sound public policy, and supported by valuable consideration — with the covenant disfavored as a restraint of trade and strictly construed against the employer. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act (S.C. Code Ann. § 39-8-30), a separate-consideration recital for covenants signed after employment begins, customer and employee non-solicitation scoped to the worker's own relationships, a non-compete tied to identified protectable interests and sized to the actual footprint, a time-limited confidentiality term so nondisclosure does not operate as an untimed non-compete, and tiered, self-contained step-down covenants rather than any reliance on judicial reformation, because South Carolina courts neither blue-pencil nor reform an overbroad covenant and extension past a stated end date is against public policy. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. South Carolina-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified South Carolina practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: signed_after_employment_began
fields.description: Whether this agreement is being signed after the employee's employment has already begun, rather than at hire. South Carolina requires separate consideration beyond continued at-will employment for a covenant entered after the inception of employment.
fields.default_value_rationale: Defaults to false because many agreements are signed at the outset of employment. If the agreement is signed later, switch this on and identify the separate new value exchanged for the covenants.

fields.name: separate_consideration
fields.description: The specific new value exchanged for a covenant signed after employment began — a raise, bonus, promotion, or change in status. Required to document the fifth reasonableness factor where the covenant is not signed at hire.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: A finite term is essential in South Carolina: an untimed nondisclosure provision that operates as a covenant not to compete violates public policy. 24 months is a common, conservative term for non-trade-secret confidentiality that keeps the clause from reading as an indefinite functional non-compete.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: South Carolina sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the conjunctive five-factor reasonableness test rather than a fixed number. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope, tied to the colleagues the departing worker actually worked with.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common customer non-solicit term in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, edging out 24 months by only a narrow margin. South Carolina sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the customer-goodwill interest and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete, so counsel should size the term to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months tracks the look-back window a District of South Carolina court upheld: a covenant limited to customers the employee had contact with during his last twelve months of employment withstood an overbreadth challenge.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under Alabama law an employee may agree to a geographically limited non-compete; this template recites the employee-exception basis, ties the covenant to a listed protectable interest, and biases the duration into the two-year presumptively reasonable window.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Non-competes are the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include only after confirming the restraint fits Alabama's employee exception for a specified geographic area, a competing business, and an existing employment relationship; protects a listed interest; stays within the two-year presumption; will be signed by all parties, including the employer; and does not restrain a member of a recognized profession from practicing that profession. The party seeking enforcement bears the burden of proof on every element.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a common, conservative pick rather than a South Carolina ceiling (South Carolina imposes no statutory cap on duration). Courts weigh time and place together against the employer's real legitimate interest under the five-factor test, so there is no safe-harbor number; counsel should size the default to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: noncompete_duration_stepdown
fields.description: A shorter alternative non-competition duration, written into the agreement as real agreed text so it can be enforced on its own terms if the primary duration is found overbroad.
fields.default_value_rationale: South Carolina courts will not narrow an overbroad covenant, but an alternative narrower restriction written into the original agreement can remain enforceable on its own terms. A shorter pre-agreed duration is that surviving structural protection; 6 months is a conservative step-down counsel should set to a defensible fallback.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. South Carolina imposes no statutory cap on territory, but a territorial scope is unreasonable if it covers an area broader than necessary to protect the employer's legitimate interest, and no court will shrink an overdrawn map - an overbroad territory fails the covenant whole.

fields.name: territory_stepdown
fields.description: A narrower alternative restricted territory, written into the agreement as real agreed text so it can be enforced on its own terms if the primary territory is found overbroad.
fields.default: the counties in which Employee actually provided services or serviced customers
fields.default_value_rationale: An alternative narrower territorial restriction written into the original agreement can remain enforceable even when the primary territory is overbroad, because the court enforces language the parties actually agreed to rather than inventing it. This is the only narrowing a South Carolina covenant will ever get.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Binding the restriction to named competitors is narrowing done at the drafting table — the only narrowing a South Carolina covenant will receive.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives), and it keeps the covenant from restraining ordinary public shareholding — an easy example against the harshness factor.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-south-dakota
items.description: South Dakota-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the SDCL chapter 53-9 statutory whitelist: confidentiality and trade-secret protection, a customer non-solicitation limited to existing customers, and an optional non-compete drawn to the § 53-9-11 employee exception — the same business or profession, a specified county, first- or second-class municipality, or other specified area, a two-year ceiling measured from the date of termination of the agreement, and the like-business condition. Customer restrictions are confined to solicitation, not acceptance, and each covenant is drafted to survive a fixed-term agreement expressly. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. South Dakota-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified South Dakota practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement — the date the last party signs. It anchors the durations stated in Cover Terms.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For a South Dakota worker, restricted territory, or customer base, plan on the SDCL chapter 53-9 analysis applying whatever law the agreement names; South Dakota courts honor a choice-of-law clause only until it contravenes South Dakota public policy.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. South Dakota enforces confidentiality covenants only to the extent reasonably necessary to protect the employer's interest in confidential information, so a finite term keeps the clause from reading as a disguised work ban.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee non-solicitation covenant is included. No staged South Dakota authority addresses a worker's own promise not to recruit former colleagues, so this covenant is left off by default and, if included, is drawn narrowly.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, and it stays within the two-year ceiling South Dakota fixes for its statutory covenants — though the employee non-solicit itself is unaddressed by staged South Dakota authority, so the statutory clock is context, not a pin. 12 months is the common lighter alternative.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: South Dakota caps the existing-customer non-solicit at two years from the date of termination of the agreement. 24 months sits at the statutory ceiling; counsel may shorten it.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for identifying the existing customers covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is a common lookback for identifying the existing-customer class SDCL 53-9-11 permits restraining.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. South Dakota generally voids restraints on a lawful profession, trade, or business unless a statutory exception applies; the employee exception permits a same-business restraint only within a specified area, for up to two years from the date of termination of the agreement, while the employer carries on a like business there.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. In a void-by-default whitelist state the non-compete is the most exposed covenant; include it only after confirming the clause fits the employee exception (same business, specified area, like-business condition), the worker is an employee rather than an independent contractor, and — for a covered practitioner — the voidable-practice-restriction rule is addressed.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: South Dakota caps the employee non-compete at two years from the date of termination of the agreement. 24 months sits at that ceiling; a term exceeding two years falls outside the exception and into the void baseline, and the exceptions are construed narrowly.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition and customer non-solicitation restrictions. SDCL 53-9-11 supplies the vocabulary: a specified county, first- or second-class municipality, or other specified area in which the employer continues to carry on a like business.
fields.default: the specified county, first- or second-class municipality, or other specified area in which Employer carries on a like business
fields.default_value_rationale: Tracks the SDCL 53-9-11 statutory vocabulary. A territory that is unspecified, unbounded, or detached from where the employer actually carries on a like business does not fit the narrowly construed exception; counsel should name the actual county, municipality, or specified area.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the same business or profession as that of the employer that constitutes competition under SDCL 53-9-11.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. In a narrow-construction regime, binding named competitors instead of an open-ended definition removes ambiguity a court would resolve against enforcement.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives); a clause that technically forbids holding ordinary public shares restrains more than any of the statutory exceptions describes.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Kept within the two-year SDCL 53-9-11 ceiling. Because a non-investment restraint that reaches active competitive roles functions as a non-compete, it is treated on the same statutory track.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: healthcare_provider
fields.description: Whether Employee is a healthcare provider whose covenant implicates profession-specific restrictions, patient access, or continuity-of-care requirements. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role; do not infer it from the agreement's jurisdiction.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-tennessee
items.description: Tennessee-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Tennessee's common-law reasonableness regime and its 2026 statutory overlay. Covenants not to compete are enforceable only when they protect special facts over and above ordinary competition and stay reasonable in time, territory, and scope under Tennessee's common-law factors. On top of that, 2026 Tenn. Pub. Acts ch. 934 (Tenn. Code Ann. §§ 50-1-210, 50-1-211), effective July 1, 2026, voids a non-compete against an employee earning under $70,000, presumes overlong time restraints unreasonable by relationship type, and expressly preserves confidentiality and customer and employee non-solicitation covenants. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection under the Tennessee Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Tenn. Code Ann. §§ 47-25-1701 to 47-25-1709), a continued-employment consideration recital, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete drawn to the reasonableness test and the statutory threshold, the § 63-1-148 health-care-provider safe harbor with its emergency-medicine exclusion, good-faith choice of law, and discretionary judicial reformation under the rule of reasonableness that the drafting does not rely on. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: An employee non-solicitation agreement is expressly preserved from the 2026 Tennessee non-compete statute's rules, so it sits outside the statutory time presumptions and the $70,000 threshold. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, sized to the workforce actually protected.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: A client or customer non-solicitation agreement is expressly preserved from the 2026 Tennessee non-compete statute's rules. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected so the covenant stays within the preserved category rather than functioning as a non-compete.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Tennessee treats a non-compete as a disfavored restraint of trade that is not void per se and is enforceable only when it protects special facts over and above ordinary competition and is reasonable under Tennessee's common-law factors. For agreements entered into, renewed, or amended on or after July 1, 2026, the covenant is also void if the employee's annualized compensation is under $70,000, and its duration is tested against the relationship-based time presumptions in Tennessee's 2026 non-compete statute.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant, and a customer non-solicit expressly preserved by Tennessee's 2026 non-compete statute often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming special facts over and above ordinary competition exist, the employee's annualized compensation is at least $70,000 for a covenant governed by the 2026 statute, the duration is within the applicable statutory presumption window, and the Tennessee health-care-provider safe harbor and emergency-medicine exclusion are addressed.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: For a covenant governed by the 2026 statute, a court presumes a restraint against a former employee or independent contractor reasonable in time when it is two years or less, measured from the date the relationship terminates, and presumes a longer term unreasonable. 12 months sits well within that window; counsel should size it to the actual role and market rather than default to the outer bound.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Geography stays a common-law question even after the 2026 statute: the ultimate test is whether the territorial limits are greater than required to protect the employer's business interest, so a footprint drawn to where the employee actually worked or served customers is far easier to defend than one drawn from corporate ambition.

A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.

fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is reasonable under the Covenants Not to Compete Act's reasonableness test.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under the Texas Covenants Not to Compete Act, a non-compete is enforceable when it is ancillary to an otherwise enforceable agreement and contains time, geographic-area, and scope-of-activity limitations that are reasonable and no greater than necessary to protect Employer's goodwill or other business interest; this template recites the ancillary-consideration basis and keeps the duration within a commonly enforced range.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Non-competes are the most heavily scrutinized covenant; include only after confirming the covenant is ancillary to an otherwise enforceable agreement supported by consideration, the time, area, and scope limitations are reasonable and no greater than necessary, and any applicable physician-specific requirements are satisfied.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a reasonable time restraint under the Covenants Not to Compete Act's reasonableness test. The tool should not default to a longer term that a court would find greater than necessary to protect Employer's goodwill.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Narrower geographic scope is more readily found reasonable under the Covenants Not to Compete Act; a Texas court reforms rather than voids an overbroad covenant but awards only injunctive relief for any pre-reformation breach, so the territory should be drawn to its final intended scope.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives).

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-utah
items.description: Utah-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for the Utah Post-Employment Restrictions Act (Utah Code sections 34-51-101 through 34-51-301) and the common-law reasonableness test: confidentiality and trade-secret protection under the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, customer and employee non-solicitation (expressly outside the statutory non-compete definition and its one-year cap), and an optional non-compete capped at one year from separation and drawn to the four-part reasonableness test. Reflects the statutory void consequence for over-length covenants, the section 34-51-301 employer fee-shifting exposure, the sale-of-business and severance exemptions, and the 2026 healthcare-worker and veterinarian bans. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Utah-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Utah practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.description: Governing law state

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: Utah leaves confidentiality agreements outside the Noncompetition Agreement Act's non-compete definition and one-year cap, but a confidentiality obligation on non-trade-secret information still should be reasonable. A finite term such as 24 months is defensible; a perpetual lid on non-secret information invites recharacterization as a disguised restraint on working.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: A nonsolicitation agreement is expressly excluded from the Noncompetition Agreement Act's non-compete definition and one-year cap, so no statute fixes its length; it answers to common-law reasonableness. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, sized to the actual protectable interest.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: No Utah statute fixes the lookback for a covered-employee class; 12 months is the most common window and keeps the no-poach class tied to colleagues the departing worker actually worked with, consistent with common-law reasonableness.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: A nonsolicitation agreement sits outside the Noncompetition Agreement Act's non-compete definition and one-year cap; its length is governed by common-law reasonableness rather than a statutory figure. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and a common, defensible term.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: No Utah statute fixes the customer lookback; 12 months is the most common window and keeps the covered-customer class tied to relationships the worker could actually carry away, which is what Utah common law protects.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under the Post-Employment Restrictions Act a post-employment non-compete may not run more than one year from separation and is void if it does; it must also satisfy the four-part common-law reasonableness test. This template caps the term at one year and recites the reasonableness basis.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. A Utah non-compete carries a two-layer burden: the statutory one-year ceiling with a void consequence for over-length covenants and the full common-law reasonableness test. A failed enforcement action can also shift the employee's fees, costs, and actual damages onto the employer. Include only after confirming a genuine protectable interest, a term within one year, and that the worker is not a healthcare worker or a veterinarian subject to the 2026 bans.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: The Noncompetition Agreement Act caps a post-employment non-compete at one year from the day employment ends and makes an over-length covenant void, not merely voidable. 12 months sits at the statutory ceiling; a shorter term reduces the reasonableness challenge and the void risk. The tool must not default to a term that exceeds the one-year cap.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employer actually provides its products or services
fields.default_value_rationale: Utah tests geographic scope against the area the business actually serves: a restraint no greater than the business's footprint is generally enforceable, while a territory drawn from ambition rather than the actual market is where reasonableness challenges start.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: No Utah statute fixes a passive-investment threshold; five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives), and the carve-out keeps the restraint from reaching index funds or a few public shares that no goodwill interest justifies.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: Non-disparagement is not a statutory non-compete and is not governed by a Utah statutory figure; 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions. Carve out truthful testimony, government reporting, and protected workplace speech regardless of the term.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-vermont
items.description: Vermont-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Vermont's common-law reasonableness regime: there is no general Vermont non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on the common-law reasonableness test (enforcement is ordered unless the covenant is contrary to public policy, unnecessary for the employer's protection, or unnecessarily restrictive of the employee), framed by the courts through Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 188(1). It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the Vermont Trade Secrets Act (9 V.S.A. §§ 4602-4603), a continued-at-will-employment consideration recital, customer and employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete tied to identified protectable interests and sized to the actual footprint, an express tolling clause the drafting does not assume a court will supply, profession-specific exclusions for barbering/cosmetology trainees (26 V.S.A. § 281(c)) and lawyers (Vt. R. Prof. Conduct 5.6), healthcare-professional reasonableness scrutiny without assuming any pending legislation, and an ordinary severability clause the drafting treats as a backstop rather than a narrowing strategy because Vermont court narrowing is unsettled (the case law bars equitable rewriting of the time term while other decisions carry partial-enforcement dicta). Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Vermont-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Vermont practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Vermont sets no statutory cap on duration; enforceability turns on the holistic Andrus reasonableness test rather than a fixed number. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual protectable interest.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Vermont sets no statutory cap; a customer non-solicit maps directly onto the customer-relationship goodwill that Barnes recognizes as a protectable interest and is often the better instrument than a full non-compete. 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Vermont has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only to the extent it satisfies the common-law Andrus reasonableness test and is tied to a protectable interest rather than ordinary competition. This template recites the protectable-interest basis and sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant — Vermont courts proceed with caution before enforcing restraints against competitive employment, and the employer carries the burden of proving reasonable necessity. A customer non-solicit backstopped by the Vermont Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified protectable interest (confidential information, trade secrets, or customer goodwill) and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint. Confirm the status of pending bills H.205 and H.583 before relying on any template.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: Vermont imposes no statutory cap on duration; courts weigh time and territory together against the employer's real protectable interest under Andrus, so there is no safe-harbor number. A five-year, single-county restraint tied to the employer's actual market was upheld in Andrus, but that is a ceiling on the extreme facts, not a target. 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market rather than copy from another form.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. Vermont imposes no statutory cap on territory, but enforcement was reversed in Roy's Orthopedic (1982) where the trial court made no finding as to what place the covenant covered; a territory the contract itself leaves vague invites exactly that failure. A narrower area genuinely needed to protect customer relationships or confidential information is far easier to defend under Andrus than a statewide ban.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-virginia
items.description: Virginia-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Va. Code § 40.1-28.7:8 and Virginia's strict common-law reasonableness test: confidentiality and trade-secret protection with the statutory nondisclosure carve-out preserved, an employee non-solicitation covenant that defaults off because it is presumptively unenforceable against a protected worker, a customer non-solicitation covenant confined to the worker's own solicitation so customer-initiated business stays open, and an optional non-compete confined to the worker's own role and territory. The agreement runs the statutory worker-protection gates before any covenant, states the without-cause severance-or-disclosed-compensation economics SB 170 requires for agreements entered into, amended, or renewed on or after July 1, 2026, excludes health care professionals, and — because Virginia courts do not rewrite an overbroad covenant — draws each covenant within its enforceable scope at the outset rather than relying on judicial reformation. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Virginia-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Virginia practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.description: Effective date of this agreement — the date the last party signs. It anchors the durations stated in Cover Terms.

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For a workforce living and working in Virginia this should remain Virginia; a clause naming another state for Virginia-based workers invites a public-policy challenge as an attempted detour around Virginia's worker-protective non-compete rules.

fields.name: low_wage_threshold
fields.description: Average weekly earnings figure below which Virginia bars a non-compete for the applicable year. Reference context only — surfaced as a note beneath Cover Terms, not filled into the agreement body.
fields.default_value_rationale: $1,507.01 per week is the 2026 low-wage threshold published by the Virginia Department of Workforce Development and Advancement. The Department resets it annually, so counsel should confirm the figure for the covenant's execution year.

fields.name: without_cause_compensation
fields.description: Severance benefits or other monetary payment the employer will provide, disclosed at execution, if it discharges the employee without cause. For agreements entered into, amended, or renewed on or after July 1, 2026, SB 170 makes a non-compete unenforceable after a without-cause discharge unless the employee receives severance or other prior-disclosed monetary compensation.
fields.default: severance equal to the Employee's base salary for the length of the Restricted Period, paid over that period
fields.default_value_rationale: A base-salary continuation running the length of the restricted period is a common way to satisfy the SB 170 requirement that the covenant be paired with disclosed severance or other monetary payment; the figure is a counsel-judgment starting point because the statute fixes no amount.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. A finite term keeps the residual clause from reading as an indefinite restraint on working with what the employee knows, which matters in a state that strictly construes restraints.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee non-solicitation covenant is included. An unpublished 2026 Virginia appellate decision read the state non-compete statute to prevent enforcing an employee non-solicit against a protected worker, so this covenant is presumptively unenforceable for a low-wage or overtime-eligible employee and defaults off.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. For a worker the statute protects, the only Virginia appellate reading on point treats an employee non-solicit as within the covenant-not-to-compete ban; include it only after confirming the worker is outside the protected class.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Virginia weighs function, geography, and duration together, so the longer market term is defensible only where the other elements are tightly drawn.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements and is a common enforceable duration here. Because Virginia assesses function, geography, and duration together, a shorter term reduces the room for a court to find the covenant broader than the employer's legitimate interest requires.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Virginia law bars an employer from entering into, enforcing, or even threatening to enforce a non-compete with a low-wage employee — a class that since July 1, 2025 also covers every FLSA-overtime-eligible worker regardless of pay, and that from July 1, 2026 also covers any health care professional. For a worker outside those classes the employer must still carry Virginia's three-part reasonableness burden, and Virginia courts will not rewrite an overbroad covenant. This template recites those gates when the covenant is included.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. Even handing a form non-compete to a covered worker is a violation of the ban's threatening-to-enforce prong, carrying a two-year private action, voiding, liquidated damages, fees, and a $10,000-per-violation civil penalty. Include only after confirming the worker is not low-wage, not overtime-eligible, not (from July 1, 2026) a health care professional, and that the restraint is drawn no broader than the employer's legitimate interest requires.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements — a common, conservative pick rather than a Virginia ceiling (Virginia sets no fixed duration). Virginia never reads duration in isolation; it weighs function, geography, and duration together, so a modest period cannot redeem an immodest function or territory.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee worked or had responsibility for Employer during the last two years of employment
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to where the worker actually operated rather than the employer's full footprint. Because Virginia will not narrow an overbroad covenant (Home Paramount; Motion Control), an overbroad territory is void rather than trimmed, so the default is drawn to the worker's own working area.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming the employer's real competitors is strong evidence the covenant reaches no further than the legitimate business interest requires, which matters in Virginia because a court will not rewrite an overbroad similar-business definition into a lawful one.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-washington
items.description: Washington-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for chapter 49.62 RCW as amended by SSB 5935 (effective 2024): confidentiality and trade-secret protection, customer and employee non-solicitation held to the statutory solicitation-only definition, and an optional non-compete gated on the inflation-adjusted earnings threshold ($126,858.83 for employees / $317,147.09 for independent contractors in 2026 per the Department of Labor & Industries), the at-hire written disclosure or post-hire independent consideration requirement, the eighteen-month duration presumption, and the layoff garden-leave-pay condition. Governing law, venue, and choice of law are locked to Washington for Washington-based workers under RCW 49.62.050, and the agreement flags the June 30, 2027 near-total ban under ESHB 1155. Because RCW 49.62.080(3) penalizes judicial reformation, each covenant is drawn to the minimum the legitimate interest requires. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
