fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. Washington excludes confidentiality agreements from the non-compete definition, so a finite, information-shaped term keeps the clause inside that exclusion.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Washington excludes an agreement not to solicit the employer's employees to leave from the non-compete definition entirely, so this covenant is not subject to the eighteen-month presumption that governs non-competes; the exclusion turns on the solicitation-only shape of the clause, not its length.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements, and it sits below the eighteen-month ceiling that will govern the surviving customer non-solicitation exclusion effective June 30, 2027, so the clause remains within the narrowed exclusion after that date.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under Washington law, an employee non-compete is void and unenforceable unless the employer clears every statutory condition: timely written disclosure or independent consideration, the inflation-adjusted earnings threshold, and layoff garden-leave pay. Washington also presumes any term over eighteen months unreasonable, and the Noncompetition Agreement Act voids all non-competition covenants effective June 30, 2027 regardless of when they were signed.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. A Washington non-compete is void unless every statutory condition is satisfied and has a hard statutory expiry on June 30, 2027; include only after confirming the earnings threshold is met, the disclosure-or-consideration timing rule is satisfied, and the layoff garden-leave condition and eighteen-month presumption are addressed.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months sits well within Washington's eighteen-month presumption, which treats any longer term as unreasonable and unenforceable unless the employer rebuts the presumption by clear and convincing evidence. Because reformation of an overbroad term triggers a statutory penalty, the default stays inside the presumptively reasonable window.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area so the starting value is not an open-ended geographic restraint. Counsel should tailor the territory to the employee's role and the employer's actual market.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors reduces the risk that a court narrows it — and therefore the risk of the statutory reformation penalty.

fields.name: washington_consideration
fields.description: Washington only. For a covenant entered into after employment has commenced, the independent consideration given specifically in exchange for the restrictive covenants, recited so it is identifiable new value under Washington law. Continued at-will employment alone is not consideration for a post-hire non-compete. State the specific new value here (for example, a signing or retention bonus, equity grant, or promotion). Shown in the Washington timing and consideration recital.
fields.default: the signing or retention bonus, equity grant, promotion, or other new benefit the parties identify as given specifically in exchange for the restrictive covenants
fields.default_value_rationale: For an at-hire covenant, Washington requires only timely written disclosure, not independent consideration; for a post-hire covenant, Washington requires identifiable new value. The default is a placeholder prompting counsel to name the specific benefit, because continued employment alone will not qualify.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months stays within Washington's eighteen-month presumption, which applies because Washington treats a non-dealing clause as a non-competition covenant.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A carve-out that technically barred index funds and ordinary public shares would restrain more than any legitimate interest requires, inviting the statutory reformation penalty.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months stays within Washington's eighteen-month presumption, which applies because a covenant restraining active roles at a Competitive Business functions as a non-competition covenant.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-west-virginia
items.description: West Virginia-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for West Virginia's common-law reasonableness regime with its facial-overbreadth void trap: there is no general West Virginia non-compete statute, so every post-employment restraint stands or falls on a two-stage common-law inquiry — the employer must first show an interest requiring protection, and the restraint is then reasonable only if it is no greater than required for the employer's protection, imposes no undue hardship on the employee, and is not injurious to the public. A covenant unreasonable on its face is utterly void with no partial enforcement, so the drafting does not rely on a savings or reformation clause to cure overbreadth. It provides confidentiality and trade-secret protection backstopped by the West Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (W. Va. Code §§ 47-22-4, 47-22-7), a new-consideration recital for covenants signed after employment begins, customer restrictions drafted as true non-piracy provisions, employee non-solicitation, an optional non-compete tied to identified protectable interests and sized to the actual footprint, a sale-of-business acknowledgement under the lesser-scrutiny standard, and physician covenant limits capped at one year and thirty road miles with voidness on employer termination (W. Va. Code § 47-11E-1 to -5). Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. West Virginia-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified West Virginia practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state. For a workforce living and working in West Virginia this should remain West Virginia; the governing-law and venue choices should match where the employee actually lives and works, and any mismatch between the named state and the employee's real location is a question for counsel.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for non-trade-secret confidentiality obligations in employment agreements. In West Virginia a perpetual lid on non-secret information can operate as a practical non-compete, so ordinary confidential information carries its own finite term.

fields.name: mid_employment_consideration
fields.description: If this agreement is signed after employment has already begun, the specific new consideration exchanged for the covenants (a raise, bonus, promotion, equity grant, or term extension). Leave blank if the agreement is signed at the outset of employment.
fields.default_value_rationale: West Virginia requires new consideration for a covenant contracted after employment has commenced without restriction; continued at-will employment alone is not enough. Left blank by default because an agreement signed at the outset of employment is supported by the offer and commencement of employment; when signed later, counsel should recite the specific covenant-linked value.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: West Virginia sets no statutory cap on duration for a general-employment covenant; enforceability turns on the Reddy reasonableness inquiry rather than a fixed number, and no staged West Virginia case sets a separate standard for employee non-solicits. 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative, and counsel should size the term to the employer's actual workforce-stability interest and keep it a modest restraint rather than a de facto hiring ban.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for employee non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: West Virginia sets no statutory cap; a customer restriction earns its lighter non-piracy treatment only while it follows customers and confidential information rather than a map, and ordinarily carries no territorial limit. 12 months is a common, conservative duration counsel should size to the actual customer relationships protected.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the most common lookback period for customer non-solicitation scope.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. West Virginia has no general non-compete statute; a non-compete is enforceable only where the employer first shows an interest requiring protection and the restraint satisfies the three-part Reddy reasonableness inquiry. A covenant unreasonable on its face — excessively broad time or area, or an apparent purpose of intimidation — is utterly void with no partial enforcement, so this template sizes the restraint to the employer's actual footprint at signing rather than relying on judicial narrowing.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant and the one exposed to the facial-voidness trap; a customer non-piracy provision backstopped by the West Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act often protects the employer as well. Include only after confirming an identified protectable interest (trade secrets, customer lists, customer goodwill, or unusual employer-funded training — never protection from ordinary competition) and sizing the duration and territory to the actual footprint the employer can defend at signing.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: West Virginia imposes no statutory cap on duration for a general-employment non-compete; time and territory are weighed together against the employer's real protectable interest under the common-law reasonableness test, and an excessively broad time limit can make the whole covenant void on its face rather than be trimmed. 12 months is a common, conservative default counsel should size to the actual role and market. A physician covenant is separately capped at one year.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: Tied to the employee's actual service area. West Virginia imposes no statutory cap on territory for a general-employment covenant, but an excessively broad area limitation is half of the facial-unreasonableness screen — a territory drawn around ambition rather than actual business reach risks voiding the covenant outright rather than inviting a court to shrink it. A physician covenant is separately capped at thirty road miles from the primary place of practice.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Narrowing the restriction to named competitors strengthens enforceability.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A clause that forbids ordinary index funds and public shares reads as gratuitous overbreadth — the kind of grasping detail that can make a covenant look designed to intimidate rather than protect.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction

fields.name: sale_of_business_covenant
fields.description: Whether this covenant is given by Employee in connection with a genuine sale of a business or practice to Employer, rather than solely as a condition of employment.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. West Virginia applies the rule of reason with lesser scrutiny to a covenant ancillary to a genuine business sale, because the seller is paid for the goodwill the covenant protects; but that breathing room belongs to the deal and does not travel to a covenant a worker signs as a condition of employment. Set true only where the covenant genuinely rides a sale and is papered in the transaction documents; a sale-related covenant routed through an employment agreement risks the stricter employment-side framework.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.name: worker_is_physician
fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-restrictive-covenant-wisconsin
items.description: Wisconsin-specific employee restrictive covenant agreement built for Wis. Stat. § 103.465, where a covenant is enforceable only if reasonably necessary to protect the employer, a five-factor reasonableness test governs every post-employment restraint, and an unreasonable restraint is void in full with no judicial blue-penciling. Confidentiality and trade-secret protection scoped to protectable information, customer and employee non-solicitation (the employee no-poach limited to actual working relationships), an optional non-compete drawn to the five-factor reasonableness standard, a consideration recital resting on genuine forbearance, no extension-during-breach tolling that would void the restraint, Wisconsin governing law rather than a foreign choice-of-law clause, and divisible covenants in place of reliance on reformation. Drafted as a starting point for licensed counsel review.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Wisconsin-specific analysis informed by the quote-verified Wisconsin practice note. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: employer_name
fields.description: Legal name of the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_name
fields.description: Full name of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employer_signatory_title
fields.description: Title of the authorized signatory signing for the employer

fields.name: employee_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the employee

fields.name: employee_title
fields.description: Employee job title or position (optional)

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Effective date of this agreement

fields.name: governing_law
fields.description: Governing law state
fields.default_value_rationale: Wisconsin work should be governed by Wisconsin law because Wisconsin case law can reject a foreign choice-of-law clause selected to obtain more covenant-friendly treatment when Wisconsin public policy controls employee restrictive covenants.

fields.name: confidentiality_trade_secret_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for trade secrets

fields.name: confidentiality_other_duration
fields.description: Duration of confidentiality obligations for non-trade-secret information
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is an editorial finite term for non-trade-secret confidential information. A finite term helps avoid turning ordinary business information into an indefinite restraint; Wisconsin authority warns that a perpetual limit on ordinary business information can be treated as an unenforceable restraint.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the employee no-hire / non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama's only category for restraints touching another party's workers is the no-hire exception for workers in a position uniquely essential to the management, organization, or service of the business.

fields.name: employee_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of employee non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months matches the modal employee non-solicit term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements; 12 months is the common lighter alternative. Either term must still read as a reasonable, fixed time limit under Wisconsin's five-factor reasonableness test, and counsel should confirm the term against the protected interest and the worker's role.

fields.name: covered_employee_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which employees are covered by the non-solicitation restriction.
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is an editorial look-back window. It ties the covered class to colleagues the departing worker actually worked with, supervised, or gained material information about, avoiding an all-workforce no-poach clause that Wisconsin authority treats as overbroad.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_included
fields.description: Whether the current-customer non-solicitation covenant is included. Alabama gives this covenant its own category, limited to current customers.

fields.name: customer_nonsolicit_duration
fields.description: Duration of customer/partner non-solicitation restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months is the lighter side of the near-even 12/24-month split observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements. The duration must be a reasonable, definite time limit, and Wisconsin sets no statutory figure, so counsel should size the term to the protected customer relationships.

fields.name: covered_customer_period
fields.description: Lookback period for determining which customers are covered by the non-solicitation and non-dealing restrictions.
fields.default_value_rationale: Bounding the covered-customer class to customers the worker actually served or learned protected information about during a 12-month stated window keeps the covenant measurable as written. The 12-month window is an editorial scoping choice, not a statutory number.

fields.name: noncompete_included
fields.description: Whether the non-competition covenant is included. Under Wisconsin's restrictive-covenant statute, a non-compete is lawful and enforceable only if the restrictions imposed are reasonably necessary for the protection of the employer, and it must satisfy all five reasonableness prerequisites; a failure on any single factor voids the restraint in full with no judicial narrowing.
fields.default_value_rationale: Conservative default. The non-compete is the most heavily scrutinized covenant and the least forgiving: because an unreasonable restraint is void even as to the part that would have been reasonable, include it only after confirming a protectable interest and a reasonable time, territory, and scope under Wisconsin's five-factor reasonableness test.

fields.name: noncompete_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-competition restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: 12 months matches the modal non-compete term observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements. A reasonable time limit is part of Wisconsin's five-factor reasonableness test, and the period must read as a fixed and definite stretch of time. Wisconsin sets no statutory ceiling, so counsel must justify the term against the protectable interest because a court cannot shorten an overlong one.

fields.name: territory
fields.description: Geographic scope of the non-competition restriction
fields.default: the geographic area in which Employee provided services
fields.default_value_rationale: A reasonable territorial limit is part of Wisconsin's five-factor reasonableness test. Tying the territory to where the employee actually operated gives the geography a defensible basis; an everywhere-the-company-does-business territory risks voiding the whole covenant because Wisconsin does not let a court rewrite an overbroad employee restraint to a narrower lawful territory.

fields.name: competitive_business_definition
fields.description: Description of the business activities that constitute competition with the employer.

fields.name: specified_competitors
fields.description: Optional named list of specific competitors. Naming real competitors is the only narrowing a Wisconsin non-compete will ever get, because Wisconsin does not let a court supply a narrower competitor list later.
fields.default_value_rationale: Leave this optional field blank unless counsel identifies specific competitors that should narrow the non-compete. The blank default avoids inventing competitor names.

fields.name: nondealing_included
fields.description: Whether the no-business-with-covered-customers covenant is included. A non-dealing covenant has no named Alabama category of its own, so it must be drafted to fit inside the current-customer non-solicit exception or it is exposed to Alabama's void rule for restraints outside the listed categories.
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Included

fields.name: nondealing_duration
fields.description: Duration of the no-business-with-covered-customers restriction
fields.display_label: No Business with Covered Customers Duration
fields.default_value_rationale: Barring the worker from serving a covered customer even on the customer's own initiative restrains accepting work, not just chasing it, and weighs on the harsh-or-oppressive reasonableness factor; a definite, reasonable term tied to a tight customer class is what keeps the restraint defensible.

fields.name: passive_public_holdings_threshold
fields.description: Maximum ownership percentage of publicly traded securities permitted under the Passive Public Holdings carveout.
fields.default_value_rationale: Five percent of any class of publicly traded securities is the modal passive-investment carve-out threshold observed in benchmarked, publicly-filed employee agreements that include the carve-out (lower 1-3 percent thresholds are the common tighter alternatives). A clause that forbids holding ordinary public shares restrains the worker far beyond any protectable interest and feeds the harsh-or-oppressive reasonableness factor.

fields.name: noninvestment_included
fields.description: Whether the non-investment covenant is included. A non-investment covenant has no named Alabama category; one genuine use is restraining a licensed professional's business conduct outside the practice of the profession, which sits outside Alabama's professional exemption.

fields.name: noninvestment_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-investment restriction
fields.default_value_rationale: An investment restraint with indefinite reach adds exactly the harshness the reasonableness factors count against the covenant; a definite, reasonable term paired with the Passive Public Holdings carve-out keeps it within a defensible scope.

fields.name: nondisparagement_duration
fields.description: Duration of non-disparagement obligation (measured from termination)
fields.default_value_rationale: 24 months is common for employment non-disparagement provisions.

fields.description: Whether Employee is a physician. Set this explicitly from Employee's licensed role to include physician-specific terms. For Indiana, the applicable provisions vary by execution date and employer type, and may require a release buyout term or exclude the non-compete entirely.

items.name: openagreements-stockholder-consent-safe
items.description: Action by written consent of the stockholders of a Delaware corporation approving the issuance of one or more Simple Agreements for Future Equity (SAFEs).
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Drafting structure informed by publicly available Series Seed SAFE stockholder consent materials. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: company_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the company

fields.name: effective_date
fields.description: Date the stockholder consent is executed

fields.name: purchase_amount
fields.description: Aggregate SAFE purchase amount (for example, "500,000")

fields.name: stockholders
fields.description: Stockholders signing the consent

items.name: name
items.description: Full name of a stockholder signing the consent

items.name: openagreements-stockholder-ratifying-consent
items.display_name: Stockholder Consent Ratifying a Defective Corporate Act
items.description: Action by written consent of the stockholders of a Delaware corporation approving the board's ratification of a defective corporate act under Section 204 of the Delaware General Corporation Law — approving the specifically identified act, and approving any related charter amendment or certificate of validation the cure requires.
items.attribution_text: Authored by OpenAgreements contributors. Drafting structure informed by publicly available Delaware Section 204 ratification practice. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

fields.name: company_name
fields.description: Full legal name of the Delaware corporation

fields.name: consent_date
fields.description: Date the stockholder consent is effective and signed

fields.name: board_consent_reference
fields.description: Reference to the board ratifying consent being approved (for example, "the Action by Written Consent of the Board of Directors dated March 1, 2026")

fields.name: defective_acts
fields.description: Description of the defective corporate act ratified by the Board and approved here (for example, "the issuance of 500,000 shares of Common Stock to Jane Founder")

fields.name: approving_percentage
fields.description: The percentage of outstanding voting stock signing this consent, computed excluding the putative shares being ratified (for example, "a majority")

fields.name: stockholder_signatories
fields.description: Stockholders signing the consent

items.name: name
items.description: Full name of a stockholder signing the consent
